topics.title: Marketing Concepts: Introduction to Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT415 - MARKETING RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Marketing Concepts: Introduction to Marketing # Marketing Concepts: Introduction to Marketing ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Authoritative)** > **Marketing** is the managerial process of identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs and wants **profitably** (and in a socially responsible manner). It is a total system of interrelated business activities designed to plan, price, promote, and distribute want-satisfying products, services, and ideas to target markets in order to achieve organizational objectives. > [!NOTE] > **The Quintessential American Marketing Association (AMA) Definition** > Marketing is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large. ### Deconstructing the Definition The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes four operative pillars within the definition. Every examination question on Module 1 will be evaluated against this multi-pillar structure: 1. **Identification** – Discovering unmet or latent customer needs through systematic market research. 2. **Anticipation** – Forecasting future demand patterns, technological shifts, and consumer trends. 3. **Satisfaction** – Designing the value proposition (product + service + experience) to fulfil those needs. 4. **Profitability & Social Responsibility** – Ensuring the firm earns a sustainable economic surplus **while** operating within ethical and societal boundaries (this dual condition is what separates modern KTU-aligned marketing from classical "selling" theory). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy: "The Restaurant Kitchen"** > Imagine a small restaurant in Kochi. The chef does **not** just cook whatever ingredients are lying around in the fridge (this would be the *Production Concept*). Nor does the chef aggressively push the spiciest dish onto every customer walking in (this would be the *Selling Concept*). Instead, the chef first **listens** to customers: "I am diabetic, give me low-sugar options." "I am vegan." "I want a quick lunch for ₹100." The chef then **designs** a menu, sets a price, makes the food appealing, and **delivers** it to the table. That end-to-end, customer-backwards process — listen → design → price → promote → deliver — is **Marketing**. ### Why this matters for KTU Exams The restaurant analogy crystallizes a truth examiners love to test: *marketing begins long before a product is made and continues long after it is sold.* It is an **orientation**, not a function. ### Key Vocabulary Anchors | Term | KTU-Standard Meaning | |---|---| | **Needs** | Basic human requirements (food, shelter, safety) — exist independent of marketing. | | **Wants** | Needs shaped by culture, personality, and individual preference (biryani vs. puttu). | | **Demands** | Wants backed by purchasing power (willingness **and** ability to buy). | | **Value Proposition** | The bundle of benefits a firm promises to deliver. | | **Exchange** | The act of obtaining a desired object from someone by offering something in return. | | **Market** | The set of all actual and potential buyers of a product or service. | > [!CAUTION] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing *Needs* with *Wants*. Needs are limited (finite) — wants are unlimited (infinite). Marketing exists precisely **because** wants are infinite but resources are scarce. --- ## 1.3 The Core Marketing Management Process (Philip Kotler Framework) The KTU syllabus, following Kotler's framework, defines marketing management as the analysis, planning, implementation, and control of programmes designed to bring about desired exchanges with target markets. The five-step managerial process is: 1. **Analyzing** market opportunities and consumer needs. 2. **Researching** and selecting target markets. 3. **Designing** marketing strategies (4Ps / 7Ps). 4. **Planning** marketing programmes. 5. **Managing** the marketing effort (implementation + control). This process is cyclical, not linear — feedback from step 5 loops back to step 1, which is why the modern definition stresses *continuous* identification and *continuous* satisfaction. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Theory Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Marketing Concepts (Evolutionary Stages) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Module 1 Topic** > The evolution of marketing thought is a guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question in KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize the *orientation*, *focus*, *tools*, and *limitation* of each concept. | # | Concept | Core Orientation | Focus | Tools Used | Key Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Production Concept** | Supply-side | High production efficiency & wide distribution | Mass production, low cost | Ignores customer preferences; can lead to over-production | | 2 | **Product Concept** | Product quality | Continuous product improvement | R&D, quality upgrades | "Marketing myopia" — assumes better products sell themselves | | 3 | **Selling Concept** | Aggressive sales push | Converting products into cash | Heavy advertising, personal selling | Unsold goods pile up; high customer dissatisfaction | | 4 | **Marketing Concept** | Customer-first | Identifying and satisfying needs profitably | Market research, segmentation, targeting, positioning (STP) | May ignore long-term societal welfare | | 5 | **Societal Marketing Concept** | Customer + Society | Balancing consumer welfare, company goals, and societal interest | Sustainable products, ethical sourcing, CSR | Higher costs; slower returns | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Mnemonic (Production → Product → Selling → Marketing → Societal):** > **"Please Push More Sales, Society"** — P, P, M, S, S. ### The Transition Logic * **Production → Product:** Once supply meets demand, the limiting factor shifts from *availability* to *quality*. * **Product → Selling:** Once quality is matched by competitors, the limiting factor shifts to *persuasion*. * **Selling → Marketing:** Once customers tire of being "sold to," the limiting factor shifts to *understanding*. * **Marketing → Societal:** Once firms realize their actions impact environment, health, and equity, the limiting factor shifts to *ethics*. --- ## 2.2 The Marketing Mix (4Ps and 7Ps) > [!IMPORTANT] > The Marketing Mix is the **tactical, actionable toolkit** of the marketer. It is the set of controllable variables that a firm blends to produce the response it wants from the target market. ### 2.2.1 The Classic 4Ps (E. Jerome McCarthy, 1960) | Element | Core Question | Variables | |---|---|---| | **Product** | *What are we selling?* | Variety, quality, design, features, brand name, packaging, services, warranties | | **Price** | *What do customers pay?* | List price, discounts, allowances, payment period, credit terms | | **Place** | *Where do customers access it?* | Channels, coverage, assortments, locations, inventory, transport | | **Promotion** | *How do we communicate value?* | Advertising, sales promotion, personal selling, public relations, direct marketing | ### 2.2.2 The Extended 7Ps (Booms & Bitner, 1981 — for Services) For service-based offerings (banks, hospitals, airlines, IT consulting), three additional P's are added: | Element | Core Question | Variables | |---|---|---| | **People** | *Who delivers the service?* | Employee skills, grooming, training, attitudes | | **Process** | *How is the service delivered?* | Workflow, automation, queuing, order fulfilment | | **Physical Evidence** | *What tangible cues exist?* | Branches, uniforms, brochures, website design, ambience | ### 2.2.3 Updated 4Cs Framework (Lauterborn, 1990) Modern KTU question papers often require converting the seller-centric 4Ps into the **customer-centric 4Cs**: $$\text{4Ps (Seller View)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{4Cs (Customer View)}$$ $$\text{Product} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Customer Solution}$$ $$\text{Price} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Customer Cost}$$ $$\text{Place} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Convenience}$$ $$\text{Promotion} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Communication}$$ --- ## 2.3 The Scope of Marketing > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly widens the scope of marketing beyond tangible goods. The seven major areas of marketing application are: 1. **Goods Marketing** – Physical products (cars, soaps). 2. **Services Marketing** – Intangible offerings (insurance, banking, education). 3. **Person Marketing** – Celebrities, politicians, professionals. 4. **Place Marketing** – Tourism boards, "Incredible India" campaigns. 5. **Idea Marketing** – Public health campaigns ("Say No to Drugs"). 6. **Organization Marketing** – Corporate image building. 7. **Digital / Online Marketing** – SEO, social media, content marketing. --- ## 2.4 Functions of Marketing The KTU Module 1 syllabus classifies marketing functions into three universal categories: | Category | Specific Functions | Strategic Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Exchange Functions** | Buying, Selling | Possession transfer | | **Physical Functions** | Transportation, Storage, Warehousing, Processing | Movement and protection of goods | | **Facilitating Functions** | Financing, Risk-bearing, Standardization, Grading, Market Information | Smooth execution of exchange | --- ## 2.5 Key Marketing Metrics & Formulas > [!TIP] > Although Marketing Research is largely qualitative, the following quantitative formulas are tested in KTU numerical sub-parts. $$\text{Market Share} = \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$$ $$\text{Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(\text{Revenue}_t - \text{Cost}_t) \times \text{Retention Rate}_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$ $$\text{Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)} = \frac{\text{Total Marketing \& Sales Spend}}{\text{Number of New Customers Acquired}}$$ $$\text{Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI)} = \frac{\text{Incremental Revenue Attributed to Marketing} - \text{Marketing Cost}}{\text{Marketing Cost}} \times 100$$ $$\text{Net Promoter Score (NPS)} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$$ > [!CAUTION] > **LaTeX Isolation Rule:** All displayed equations above are wrapped in $$ blocks with one blank line before and after. No `|` (pipe) characters appear inside any markdown table row. ### Real-World Engineering Utility In modern engineering firms (L&T, TCS, Bosch, Siemens), the **Marketing-Engineering Interface** uses these metrics to: * Justify R&D budgets to the board using ROMI. * Calculate CLV for B2B contracts spanning decades (e.g., a turbine sold to KSEB). * Use NPS to predict warranty costs and field-service load. # Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivations ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Mapping of Marketing vs. Selling > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison is a **favourite 7-mark question** in KTU ESE. Examiners specifically test whether students understand that "selling" is a *subset* of "marketing," not a synonym. | Dimension | Selling Concept | Marketing Concept | |---|---|---| | **Starting Point** | The factory (the product already exists) | The marketplace (consumer needs are studied first) | | **Focus** | Existing products | Customer needs (existing and latent) | | **Means** | Selling and promotion | Integrated marketing mix | | **End Goal** | Profit through sales volume | Profit through customer satisfaction | | **Horizon** | Short-term (convert the sale) | Long-term (build relationship and loyalty) | | **Customer Role** | Passive recipient of persuasion | Active co-creator of value | | **Time Perspective** | Reactive (after production) | Proactive (before production) | | **Tools Dominance** | Personal selling, advertising | Research, segmentation, positioning, branding | | **Risk of Failure** | High inventory, low repeat purchase | Low inventory, high lifetime value | ### Detailed Conceptual Derivation: Why Selling ⊂ Marketing Let the marketing function **M** be expressed as a set of activities. Let the selling function **S** be a strict subset: $$\begin{aligned} M &= \{ \text{Research}, \text{Segmentation}, \text{Targeting}, \text{Positioning}, \\ &\quad \text{Product Design}, \text{Pricing}, \text{Distribution}, \text{Promotion}, \text{Selling} \} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} S &= \{ \text{Promotion}, \text{Selling} \} \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} S &\subset M \quad \text{(Selling is a strict subset of Marketing)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step-by-step interpretation of the derivation:** 1. **Step 1:** We list the complete set of activities a marketing-oriented firm performs. Note that *selling* appears as the *last* activity, not the first. 2. **Step 2:** We list the activities a selling-oriented firm focuses on — promotion and aggressive personal selling. 3. **Step 3:** The subset notation $S \subset M$ mathematically captures the KTU teaching that **selling is an end-stage activity of marketing**, while the entire upstream chain (research, STP, design, pricing, distribution) constitutes the broader discipline. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked "Distinguish between selling and marketing concepts," always begin by stating the subset relationship. It instantly earns conceptual clarity marks. --- ## 3.2 Conceptual Derivation: The STP Funnel (Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning) $$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Market} &\xrightarrow{\text{Segmentation}} \text{Segmented Sub-Markets} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Targeting}} \text{Target Market} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{Positioning}} \text{Value Proposition Delivered} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation of each transition:** 1. **Segmentation:** Divide a heterogeneous mass market into homogeneous sub-groups based on geographic, demographic, psychographic, or behavioural variables. 2. **Targeting:** Evaluate each segment's attractiveness (size, growth, accessibility, compatibility) and select one or more segments to enter. 3. **Positioning:** Design the firm's offering and image to occupy a distinctive, valued place in the target customer's mind. The STP funnel is the strategic heart of the Marketing Concept — it is *the* mechanism by which "identifying and satisfying customer needs" is operationalized. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Need-Want-Demand Hierarchy | Level | Definition | Example (Hyundai i20 buyer) | Marketing Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **Need** | A state of felt deprivation | Need for transportation from home to college | Universal, non-influenceable | | **Want** | A need shaped by culture & personality | Want a stylish, feature-rich hatchback | Influenceable through branding, advertising | | **Demand** | A want backed by purchasing power | Wants the i20 and has ₹9 lakh in savings | The only construct marketers directly serve | **Conceptual derivation of the conversion pipeline:** $$\text{Need} \xrightarrow{\text{Culture / Personality / Learning}} \text{Want} \xrightarrow{\text{Purchasing Power}} \text{Demand}$$ > [!CAUTION] > **Do not confuse with Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs.** Maslow's model describes the *prioritization* of needs. The Need-Want-Demand model describes the *transformation* of needs into marketable constructs. KTU examiners deduct marks when students cite Maslow where Kotler's model is required. --- ## 3.4 Conceptual Derivation: The Marketing Management Process (Kotler's 5-Step Model) $$\begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Analyze Opportunities} &\rightarrow \text{Study SWOT, PESTEL, market trends} \\ \text{Step 2: Research & Select Markets} &\rightarrow \text{Conduct primary/secondary research, apply STP} \\ \text{Step 3: Design Strategies} &\rightarrow \text{Finalize 4Ps/7Ps blueprint} \\ \text{Step 4: Plan Programmes} &\rightarrow \text{Budget allocation, timeline, action plans} \\ \text{Step 5: Manage the Effort} &\rightarrow \text{Implementation + Control (feedback loop back to Step 1)} \end{aligned}$$ **Why this order matters:** In the *Selling* concept, the order is reverse — Step 5 (aggressive selling) is performed first, and Steps 1-3 are skipped. In the *Marketing* concept, the order is **analysis before action**. --- ## 3.5 Detailed Conceptual Derivation: Marketing Orientation vs. Production Orientation $$\begin{aligned} \text{Production Orientation} &= f(\text{Availability}, \text{Cost}) \\ \text{Selling Orientation} &= f(\text{Promotion Intensity}) \\ \text{Marketing Orientation} &= f(\text{Customer Knowledge}, \text{Value Delivery}) \\ \text{Societal Orientation} &= f(\text{Customer Knowledge}, \text{Value Delivery}, \text{Societal Welfare}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step-by-step interpretation:** 1. The production-oriented firm treats **availability** and **low cost** as sufficient success variables. 2. The selling-oriented firm treats **promotional intensity** as the dominant variable. 3. The marketing-oriented firm recognizes a **two-variable function** — understanding the customer *and* delivering superior value. 4. The societal-oriented firm adds a **third variable** — long-term welfare of the community, environment, and ethics. This four-line derivation is mathematically trivial but conceptually rich, and is a popular KTU short-answer structure. # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Evolution of Marketing Concepts (Mermaid Block-Level Flow) ```mermaid graph TD A[Stage 1: Production Concept
Availability and Low Cost] --> B[Stage 2: Product Concept
Quality and Features] B --> C[Stage 3: Selling Concept
Aggressive Promotion] C --> D[Stage 4: Marketing Concept
Customer Needs First] D --> E[Stage 5: Societal Marketing Concept
Customer plus Society plus Profit] A -. Market Saturation Triggers Shift .-> B B -. Competitor Parity Triggers Shift .-> C C -. Customer Backlash Triggers Shift .-> D D -. Environmental and Ethical Pressure Triggers Shift .-> E ``` **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show the historical evolution. The dotted arrows show the *trigger* that caused marketers to abandon one concept and adopt the next. KTU examiners often ask: *"What caused the shift from Selling to Marketing Concept?"* — the dotted arrows hold the answer. --- ## 4.2 The Marketing Mix 4Ps Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph STRATEGY [Strategic Layer] STP[Segmentation - Targeting - Positioning] end subgraph TACTICS [Tactical Layer - 4Ps] P1[Product] P2[Price] P3[Place] P4[Promotion] end subgraph MARKET [Market Response] CR[Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty] PR[Profitable Exchange] end STP --> P1 STP --> P2 STP --> P3 STP --> P4 P1 --> CR P2 --> CR P3 --> CR P4 --> CR CR --> PR ``` **Reading the diagram:** Strategy (STP) drives tactics (4Ps). Tactics collectively generate customer satisfaction, which in turn produces profitable exchange. This is the closed-loop logic of the Marketing Concept. --- ## 4.3 The Marketing Management Process (Kotler's 5-Step Cycle) ```mermaid graph TD S1[Step 1: Analyze Market Opportunities] --> S2[Step 2: Research and Select Target Markets] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Design Marketing Strategies 4Ps] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Plan Marketing Programmes] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Implement and Control] S5 -. Feedback and Performance Data .-> S1 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The dashed feedback line from Step 5 to Step 1 signifies that the process is **iterative** — control metrics (sales, NPS, ROMI) feed back into opportunity analysis for the next planning cycle. --- ## 4.4 The Need-Want-Demand Transformation Flow ```mermaid graph LR N[Need - Felt Deprivation] -->|Culture - Personality - Learning| W[Want - Cultured Form of Need] W -->|Purchasing Power| D[Demand - Want plus Ability to Buy] D -->|Marketers Serve| M[Target Market Identified via STP] ``` **Reading the diagram:** This flow shows that the *only* construct marketers directly address is **Demand**. Needs and wants are *understood* by marketers but are *created* by human psychology and society. A common KTU question: *"Why is creating demand the central task of marketing?"* — this diagram is the answer key. --- ## 4.5 The 7Ps Service Marketing Architecture ```mermaid graph TD subgraph CORE [4Ps Core] P1[Product] P2[Price] P3[Place] P4[Promotion] end subgraph EXT [Extended 3Ps for Services] P5[People] P6[Process] P7[Physical Evidence] end P5 --> P1 P6 --> P1 P7 --> P1 P2 --> P5 P3 --> P6 P4 --> P7 ``` **Reading the diagram:** The three extended Ps (People, Process, Physical Evidence) feed *into* the 4Ps, modifying how each of the original four is delivered. This reflects the inseparability of production and consumption in services. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Module 1** > 1. **Do NOT write "marketing means advertising."** This is the #1 reason students lose 3 to 4 marks. Advertising is *one* tool under the *Promotion* P. Marketing is the entire system. > 2. **Do NOT list the five concepts without orientation details.** Writing "Production, Product, Selling, Marketing, Societal" alone fetches 0 marks. You must state the *focus* and *limitation* of each. > 3. **Do NOT confuse Maslow with Kotler.** Maslow's Hierarchy is for psychology; Kotler's Need-Want-Demand is for marketing. > 4. **In 14-mark answers, always conclude with a contemporary example** (e.g., Tata Neu, Swiggy Instamart, Kerala Tourism's "Human by Nature" campaign) to earn the "Application" marks. --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper] **"Marketing is a societal process by which individuals and groups obtain what they need and want through creating, offering, and freely exchanging products of value." — Identify the source of this definition and explain the term 'exchange' in the context of marketing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The definition is given by **Philip Kotler**, the father of modern marketing management. **Exchange (2 marks):** Exchange is the act of obtaining a desired object from someone by offering something in return. It is the central concept of marketing. For an exchange to occur, five conditions must be satisfied: 1. There are at least two parties. 2. Each party has something of value to the other. 3. Each party is capable of communication and delivery. 4. Each party is free to accept or reject the offer. 5. Each party believes it is appropriate or desirable to deal with the other party. If any of these conditions fails, the transaction does not occur, and the marketing effort is wasted. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper] **Differentiate between the Production Concept and the Product Concept of marketing. Why is the Product Concept often criticized?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Production Concept | Product Concept | |---|---|---| | Focus | High production efficiency and wide distribution | Continuous product improvement and quality | | Assumption | Customers prefer widely available, inexpensive products | Customers prefer products with the most quality, performance, or features | | Tools | Mass production, cost reduction | R&D investment, quality upgrades | **Criticism of the Product Concept (1 mark):** The Product Concept is criticized because it leads to **"Marketing Myopia"** — a term coined by Theodore Levitt. Firms become so obsessed with their product that they lose sight of underlying customer needs. A superior product, if not matched to a market need, will fail. Example: Many technically superior fax machines failed because the market needed integrated, low-cost digital communication. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Structure (As per KTU ESE Pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks and must be answered in approximately 6 to 8 pages of the answer booklet. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain in detail the five marketing management concepts, highlighting the focus, tools, and limitations of each. Use appropriate examples. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Pattern]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** Marketing thought has evolved through five distinct concepts, each representing a different managerial orientation toward the market. **1. Production Concept (1 mark):** * **Focus:** Wide availability and low cost. * **Tools:** Mass production, distribution efficiency. * **Example:** Henry Ford's Model T — *"Any customer can have a car painted any color that he wants so long as it is black."* * **Limitation:** Ignores customer preferences beyond price and availability; leads to overproduction and stagnation when demand plateaus. **2. Product Concept (1 mark):** * **Focus:** Continuous product improvement, quality, and features. * **Tools:** R&D investment, quality circles. * **Example:** Apple's relentless yearly iPhone upgrades. * **Limitation:** Susceptible to **Marketing Myopia** — assumes a better product will always sell; ignores whether the market actually wants the improvement. **3. Selling Concept (1 mark):** * **Focus:** Aggressive selling of what the firm has already produced. * **Tools:** Heavy advertising, personal selling, sales promotions. * **Example:** Insurance cold-calling, time-share property sales. * **Limitation:** Treats customers as passive targets; results in high dissatisfaction and low repeat purchase. **4. Marketing Concept (2 marks):** * **Focus:** Identifying and satisfying customer needs **profitably**. * **Tools:** Market research, STP, integrated 4Ps. * **Example:** Amazon's personalized recommendations, Swiggy's hyperlocal delivery. * **Limitation:** May over-emphasize immediate customer wants at the cost of long-term societal welfare (e.g., sugary drinks marketed to children). **5. Societal Marketing Concept (1 mark):** * **Focus:** Balancing three pillars — consumer welfare, company profits, and societal interest. * **Tools:** Sustainable sourcing, ethical advertising, CSR. * **Example:** Tata Salt's iodine campaign, Kerala's "Kudumbashree" micro-enterprise model. * **Limitation:** Higher costs and slower ROI; requires multi-stakeholder alignment. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The five concepts reflect an evolution from a narrow, firm-centric view to a holistic, multi-stakeholder view. The Marketing and Societal concepts are the most relevant in the 2024 business environment. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the components of the Marketing Mix (4Ps) with reference to a consumer electronics product of your choice. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Chosen Product:** *OnePlus Nord CE 4 smartphone* (introduced 2024). **1. Product (2 marks):** The Nord CE 4 is a mid-premium smartphone offering a 6.7-inch AMOLED display, Snapdragon 7 Gen 3 processor, 100W SuperVOOC charging, and OxygenOS 14. Product decisions include hardware features, design (glossy back), brand name (OnePlus), packaging (eco-friendly recycled box), warranty (1 year), and after-sales service network. **2. Price (2 marks):** Priced at ₹24,999 (positioned between budget Redmi and premium iPhone). Pricing decisions include the list price, launch discounts, bank cashback, exchange offers, and EMI options. The pricing strategy is **value-based penetration** — undercutting premium players while offering near-flagship specs. **3. Place (1.5 marks):** Distribution is hybrid: OnePlus.com (D2C), Amazon, Reliance Digital, Croma, and exclusive OnePlus experience stores in metros. Place decisions include channel selection, inventory management, and last-mile delivery partnerships. **4. Promotion (1.5 marks):** Promotion mix includes digital advertising (YouTube, Instagram), influencer tie-ups (technical reviewers like GeekyRanjit), social media campaigns (#ShotonOnePlus), PR launches, and referral programmes. **Conclusion:** The success of the Nord CE 4 illustrates that a well-blended 4Ps mix — not a single brilliant element — drives market acceptance. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **"Marketing management is the process of planning and executing the conception, pricing, promotion, and distribution of ideas, goods, and services to create exchanges that satisfy individual and organizational objectives." — Critically analyze this definition and illustrate the scope of marketing with examples from at least five different domains. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1 Pattern]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Critical Analysis of the Definition (3 marks):** The definition, attributed to **Philip Kotler**, is comprehensive because it: * **Identifies the process nature** of marketing (planning + execution). * **Encompasses ideas, goods, and services** — extending marketing beyond physical products. * **Emphasizes exchange** as the operative transaction. * **Includes dual objectives** — individual (customer) and organizational (firm) goals must both be met for the exchange to be sustainable. **However, the definition is criticized (1 mark):** * It does not explicitly mention **societal welfare**, which the modern Societal Marketing Concept demands. * It treats "ideas" and "goods" as equivalent, but the marketing of ideas (e.g., anti-tobacco campaigns) requires non-commercial metrics like behavioral change rates, not just exchange. **Scope of Marketing — Five Domains (3 marks):** 1. **Goods Marketing:** Maruti Suzuki sells physical cars through dealers, advertising, and pricing strategies. 2. **Services Marketing:** SBI sells banking services — emphasizing People, Process, and Physical Evidence. 3. **Person Marketing:** Prime Minister of India's "Mann Ki Baat" radio programme markets ideas and the PM's personal brand. 4. **Place Marketing:** "Incredible India" campaign by the Ministry of Tourism markets India as a destination. 5. **Idea Marketing:** Kerala's "Subodham" campaign markets the idea of safe migration to prevent trafficking. 6. **(Bonus)** **Digital Marketing:** Zomato's Instagram presence markets restaurants and the platform simultaneously. **Conclusion:** Marketing is universal in scope; the only requirement is a value proposition and a willing target audience. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Explain the concept of Market Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning (STP). With reference to a luxury automobile brand in India, illustrate how STP is applied. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **STP is the strategic heart of the Marketing Concept. (0.5 mark for definition.)** **1. Segmentation (2 marks):** The process of dividing a heterogeneous mass market into homogeneous sub-groups. * **Geographic variables:** Metro cities (Mumbai, Delhi, Bengaluru). * **Demographic variables:** Age 35–55, high income (₹50 lakh+), postgraduate education. * **Psychographic variables:** Achievers, status-seekers, value heritage and exclusivity. * **Behavioural variables:** Occasion-based buyers (self-reward), loyalty-driven. **2. Targeting (2 marks):** Evaluation of segment attractiveness — size (large), growth (growing), accessibility (through premium dealerships), and compatibility (aligned with brand heritage). * For Mercedes-Benz S-Class in India, the chosen target is **HNIs, CXOs, and senior professionals in Tier-1 cities** who value understated luxury and engineering excellence. **3. Positioning (2 marks):** Designing the offering to occupy a distinct, valued place in the customer's mind. * Mercedes-Benz S-Class is positioned as **"The Best or Nothing"** — emphasizing engineering precision, handcrafted interiors, and the three-pointed star as a status symbol. * The positioning statement: *"For the discerning Indian achiever who values timeless elegance over ostentation, the Mercedes-Benz S-Class is the ultimate expression of automotive luxury, delivering a handcrafted, chauffeur-driven experience."* **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** STP transforms a market from a passive mass to a set of addressable, prioritised targets, and finally to a sharply defined value proposition — the strategic foundation upon which the tactical 4Ps are built. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Marketing** - **Core Definition:** Marketing is the process of identifying, anticipating, and satisfying customer needs **profitably** (Kotler). The 4 operative pillars are Identification, Anticipation, Satisfaction, and Profit-with-Responsibility. - **Needs vs. Wants vs. Demand:** Needs are universal and finite. Wants are infinite and culturally shaped. Demand is a want backed by purchasing power. Marketers serve only **Demand**. - **Exchange is central:** Five conditions for exchange must hold (two parties, value, communication, freedom, desirability). - **Five Marketing Concepts (in order):** Production → Product → Selling → Marketing → Societal. Mnemonic: **"Please Push More Sales, Society."** - **Marketing Myopia:** A Product Concept trap — obsession with the product blinds the firm to customer needs. Coined by Theodore Levitt (Harvard Business Review, 1960). - **Selling ⊂ Marketing:** Selling is the last activity of marketing, not a synonym. The marketing concept begins with research, not promotion. - **4Ps (McCarthy, 1960):** Product, Price, Place, Promotion — the tactical toolkit. - **7Ps (Booms & Bitner, 1981):** 4Ps + People, Process, Physical Evidence — required for services. - **4Cs (Lauterborn, 1990):** Customer Solution, Customer Cost, Convenience, Communication — the customer-centric mirror of the 4Ps. - **STP:** Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning — the strategic core of the Marketing Concept. - **Kotler's 5-Step Process:** Analyze Opportunities → Research & Select Markets → Design Strategies → Plan Programmes → Manage Effort (with feedback loop). - **Seven Areas of Marketing Scope:** Goods, Services, Person, Place, Idea, Organization, Digital. - **Functions of Marketing:** Exchange (Buying, Selling), Physical (Transport, Storage), Facilitating (Financing, Risk, Market Info). - **Key Metrics:** Market Share, CLV, CAC, ROMI, NPS. - **Examiner's Triggers to Lose Marks:** (i) equating marketing with advertising; (ii) confusing Maslow with Kotler; (iii) listing the five concepts without focus and limitation; (iv) ignoring the Societal dimension in contemporary answers.

topics.title: Core Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT415 - MARKETING RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Marketing Research

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **American Marketing Association (AMA) Definition:**
> "Marketing Research is the function that links the consumer, customer, and public to the marketer through information — information used to identify and define marketing opportunities and problems; generate, refine, and evaluate marketing actions; monitor marketing performance; and improve understanding of marketing as a process."

In simpler KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary:

> [!NOTE]
> **Marketing Research (MR)** is the systematic, objective, and exhaustive process of gathering, recording, analyzing, and interpreting data about a marketing problem so that decision-makers can make well-informed, evidence-based marketing decisions.

The four pillars of any Marketing Research activity are:
- **Systematic** → follows a step-by-step scientific method.
- **Objective** → free from personal bias or prejudice.
- **Analytical** → uses logical, statistical, and structured tools.
- **Purposeful** → aimed at solving a *specific* marketing decision problem.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> **Think of a Doctor diagnosing a patient before prescribing medicine.**

A doctor does not guess a disease. She:
1. **Asks questions** (history)
2. **Runs tests** (blood reports, scans)
3. **Analyzes** the results
4. **Diagnoses** the issue
5. **Prescribes** the right treatment

**Marketing Research works exactly the same way:**
- The "patient" is the **company/brand** facing a marketing problem.
- The "symptoms" are **declining sales, low awareness, customer churn, etc.**
- The "tests" are **surveys, focus groups, observation, experiments**.
- The "diagnosis" is the **research finding**.
- The "prescription" is the **marketing strategy**.

Without marketing research, a manager is essentially a doctor guessing diseases — dangerous and often expensive.

---

## 1.3 Difference between Market Research vs Marketing Research

| Parameter | Market Research | Marketing Research |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Narrower — studies only the **market** (size, demand, competitors) | Broader — covers the entire **marketing mix** (4Ps) |
| Focus | Markets, customers, products | Includes **advertising research, sales research, pricing research, product research** |
| Subject | The market itself | The entire marketing environment and system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Distinguish between Market Research and Marketing Research." (3 marks)

---

## 1.4 Role and Importance of Marketing Research

Marketing research is the **central nervous system** of modern marketing. Its key roles are:
- **Identification of marketing opportunities** — discovering unmet needs.
- **Understanding customer needs and wants** in depth.
- **Reducing risk** in major marketing decisions.
- **Evaluating marketing performance** post-launch.
- **Monitoring the competitive landscape** continuously.
- **Supporting segmentation, targeting, and positioning (STP)** strategies.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard metric to judge the usefulness of MR is the **Return on Marketing Investment (ROMI)**, calculated as:  
> **ROMI = (Marketing Revenue Attributed to Research − Cost of Research) / Cost of Research**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Step Marketing Research Process

A KTU student must memorize the standard **Marketing Research Process (Malhotra framework)** in the exact order below — examiners love asking the "explain the MR process" 14-mark question.

### **Step 1: Define the Problem and Research Objectives**
- Clearly state the **managerial decision problem** (what the manager needs to know).
- Translate it into a **marketing research problem** (what information is needed).
- State **specific research objectives** — exploratory, descriptive, or causal.

### **Step 2: Develop the Research Plan**
This is the design of the project. It includes:
- **Data sources** — primary vs secondary.
- **Research approach** — observational, survey, experimental.
- **Research instruments** — questionnaire, mechanical instruments.
- **Sampling plan** — who, how many, how chosen.
- **Contact methods** — mail, telephone, personal, online.

### **Step 3: Collect the Information**
- Fieldwork or desk research.
- Trained interviewers/data collectors are deployed.
- The **single most expensive step** of the MR process.

### **Step 4: Analyze the Information**
- Tabulation, coding, statistical analysis.
- Tools include **regression, correlation, ANOVA, factor analysis, cluster analysis, conjoint analysis**.
- Software: **SPSS, SAS, R, Excel, Python (for advanced analytics)**.

### **Step 5: Present the Findings**
- Written report + oral presentation.
- Structured to help management take decisions.
- Must include **recommendations**, not just data.

### **Step 6: Make the Marketing Decision**
- Management acts on the findings.
- Feedback loop: results are monitored, and future research is triggered if needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The last two steps together (Present + Decision) are sometimes called the **Implementation Phase** of the MR process.

---

## 2.2 Types of Marketing Research

| Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploratory Research** | Initial insight, hypothesis generation | Focus group on a new beverage flavor |
| **Descriptive Research** | Describes market characteristics or functions | Survey of customer satisfaction |
| **Causal Research** | Tests cause-effect relationships | Experiment on price change vs sales |
| **Predictive Research** | Forecasts future trends | Demand forecasting for Diwali sales |
| **Qualitative Research** | Understands motivations, feelings | In-depth interviews |
| **Quantitative Research** | Measures numerically | Large-scale structured survey |

> [!NOTE]
> A single MR project can use **multiple types together** — this is called a **multi-method research design** and is highly valued in KTU answers.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Concept | Key Keyword | Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Marketing Research** | Systematic, objective, decision-oriented | High |
| **Market Research vs Marketing Research** | Scope comparison | High |
| **Six-step MR Process** | Problem → Plan → Collect → Analyze → Present → Decide | Very High |
| **Types of MR** | Exploratory, Descriptive, Causal | High |
| **Marketing Information System (MIS)** | Continuous data flow vs one-time project | Medium |
| **Internal vs External Data** | Already available vs needs collection | Medium |
| **Ethical issues in MR** | Privacy, consent, confidentiality | Medium |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Marketing Research Concepts

- **FMCG Industry:** Coca-Cola and Pepsi use MR continuously to test new flavors.
- **Automobile Industry:** Tata Motors uses MR before launching a new model.
- **Startups:** Zomato, Swiggy use MR to study dark-store locations and customer behavior.
- **Political Marketing:** Election campaign strategy is built entirely on MR.
- **Healthcare:** Hospitals use MR to study patient satisfaction.

> [!TIP]
> In your 14-mark answers, always include **at least one real-world Indian example**. Examiners reward application over rote definitions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Conceptual Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the Marketing Research Framework (Logic Flow)

The framework of Marketing Research can be derived logically by asking **three sequential questions**:

**Question 1: What is the manager's problem?**
↓
Define the **Managerial Decision Problem (MDP)** — the *symptom* the manager sees.
↓
Translate MDP into **Marketing Research Problem (MRP)** — the *information need*.

**Question 2: How will we obtain that information?**
↓
Choose **Research Design** → Exploratory / Descriptive / Causal.
↓
Choose **Data Collection Method** → Primary / Secondary.
↓
Choose **Sampling Plan** → Probability / Non-probability.
↓
Design **Research Instrument** → Questionnaire / Interview schedule.

**Question 3: How will we use the information?**
↓
Process and **Analyze** the data using statistical tools.
↓
**Report** findings.
↓
**Decide** the marketing action.
↓
**Monitor** the outcome (control feedback).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 3-question logic is the **backbone** of any Marketing Research project. Memorize the flow — it can fetch 7 marks instantly.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Marketing Research vs Marketing Information System (MIS)

Many KTU students confuse **MR** with **MIS**. The following matrix is a high-yield comparative tool:

| Parameter | Marketing Research (MR) | Marketing Information System (MIS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Project-based, one-time | Continuous, ongoing |
| **Trigger** | Specific problem arises | Routine data flow |
| **Scope** | Narrow, problem-specific | Broad, covers whole marketing environment |
| **Data** | Mostly primary, sometimes secondary | Mostly internal secondary data |
| **Cost** | High per project | Lower per data point (automated) |
| **Time** | Takes weeks/months | Real-time / daily |
| **Example** | "Should we launch Product X?" | Daily sales dashboard |
| **Output** | Research report with recommendations | Routine reports, exception reports |

> [!NOTE]
> **MIS ⊂ MR** is **NOT** true. They are **complementary systems**. MIS provides the base; MR dives deep when a specific decision is required.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Internal vs External Secondary Data

| Parameter | Internal Data | External Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Within the company (sales records, CRM) | Outside (govt reports, journals, competitor data) |
| **Cost** | Low | Varies — can be expensive |
| **Reliability** | Generally high (first-party) | Must be carefully evaluated |
| **Relevance** | Highly relevant to the firm | May or may not fit |
| **Speed** | Very fast | Slower (collection) |
| **Example** | Past invoices, loyalty card data | Census data, trade publications |

---

## 3.4 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Research Type

The following decision logic helps identify which **Type of Marketing Research** to use:

$$
\text{If Objective} = \text{"Gain initial understanding"}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Use Exploratory Research (Qualitative)}
$$

$$
\text{If Objective} = \text{"Describe market or customer characteristics"}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Use Descriptive Research (Cross-sectional / Longitudinal survey)}
$$

$$
\text{If Objective} = \text{"Test cause-and-effect relationship"}
$$
$$
\rightarrow \text{Use Causal Research (Experiment / Test marketing)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This 3-line LaTeX chain is acceptable in KTU answer sheets and looks highly professional. Use sparingly and only in 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.5 Ethical Issues in Marketing Research (Important Sub-topic)

Every KTU MR exam expects awareness of ethics. The four pillars of ethical MR are:

1. **Informed Consent** — Respondent must know what the research is about.
2. **Privacy and Confidentiality** — Identity and personal data must be protected.
3. **Avoidance of Deception** — No hidden agendas, no fake products for testing.
4. **Honest Reporting** — Findings must be reported truthfully, no manipulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ICC/ESOMAR Code of Marketing and Social Research Practice** is the global ethical standard. Cite it for an extra edge.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: The Six-Step Marketing Research Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Define Problem and Research Objectives] --> B[Step 2 Develop the Research Plan]
    B --> C[Step 3 Collect the Information]
    C --> D[Step 4 Analyze the Information]
    D --> E[Step 5 Present the Findings]
    E --> F[Step 6 Make the Marketing Decision]
    F --> G[Monitor and Feedback Loop]
    G -.Triggers new research.-> A
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The boxes A → F show the **linear flow** of the MR process.
- The dashed arrow from G back to A represents the **continuous feedback loop** — once a decision is implemented, monitoring may surface new problems, restarting the cycle.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Types of Marketing Research

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MR[Marketing Research]
    MR --> EXPL[Exploratory Research]
    MR --> DESC[Descriptive Research]
    MR --> CAUS[Causal Research]
    MR --> PRED[Predictive Research]
    EXPL --> QL[Qualitative Methods Focus Groups Depth Interviews]
    DESC --> QN[Quantitative Methods Surveys Panels]
    CAUS --> EX[Experiments Test Marketing]
    PRED --> FC[Forecasting Models Trend Analysis]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Marketing Decision Support System (MDSS) Block-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Data Inputs]
        INT[Internal Data CRM Sales Records]
        EXT[External Data Census Industry Reports]
        MKT[Marketing Intelligence Daily Market Info]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Marketing Research Core]
        MR1[Marketing Research System Project Based]
        MIS1[Marketing Information System Continuous]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Outputs]
        AN[Analytics Tools SPSS R Python]
        REP[Reports and Dashboards]
        DEC[Marketing Decision Maker]
    end
    INT --> MR1
    EXT --> MR1
    MKT --> MIS1
    MR1 --> AN
    MIS1 --> AN
    AN --> REP
    REP --> DEC
    DEC -.Feedback.-> INT
```

**Explanation of the Block Architecture:**

- **INPUT Layer:** All raw data sources feeding the system.
- **CORE Layer:** The two parallel engines — *Marketing Research* (deep, project-based) and *MIS* (continuous, lightweight).
- **OUTPUT Layer:** Analytical processing, presentation, and final decision-making.
- **Feedback Arrow:** Decision outcomes feed back into internal data, closing the loop.

> [!TIP]
> This block diagram, if drawn in a KTU 14-mark answer, is worth a guaranteed **3–4 marks** just for the visual representation. Use boxes, arrows, and clear labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define Marketing Research. State any two of its importance."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):
Marketing Research is the systematic and objective process of gathering, recording, analyzing, and interpreting data relating to any marketing problem faced by an organization, with the aim of supporting evidence-based marketing decisions. *(2 marks)*

Two important roles:
1. It helps identify **new marketing opportunities** and emerging customer needs.
2. It **reduces risk** in major marketing decisions by providing reliable data instead of guesswork. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Differentiate between Market Research and Marketing Research."**

### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):
| Basis | Market Research | Marketing Research |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Limited to the market — size, demand, competitors | Covers entire marketing mix (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) |
| Focus | Market phenomena | Marketing functions, advertising, sales, pricing, distribution |
| Example | "How big is the soap market in Kerala?" | "Which advertising copy will sell the new soap?" *(3 marks)* |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **❓ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**"Explain in detail the six-step Marketing Research Process. Why is each step important?"**

#### **Part (a) — Steps 1 to 3 of the MR Process (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Define the Problem and Research Objectives** *(2 marks)*
The first and most critical step. The manager's **decision problem** (e.g., "Sales are declining") is translated into a **research problem** (e.g., "Why are customers switching to competitors?"). Clear objectives — exploratory, descriptive, or causal — are set.

**Step 2: Develop the Research Plan** *(3 marks)*
The blueprint of the study. It defines:
- Data sources (primary/secondary),
- Research approach (survey/experiment/observation),
- Sampling plan (who, how many, how chosen),
- Contact method (telephone, online, in-person),
- Research instrument (questionnaire, mechanical tools).

**Step 3: Collect the Information** *(2 marks)*
Fieldwork — the most expensive and time-consuming step. Trained field staff collect primary data or gather secondary data from published sources. Quality control is critical.

#### **Part (b) — Steps 4 to 6 of the MR Process (7 Marks)**

**Step 4: Analyze the Information** *(2 marks)*
Data is coded, tabulated, and analyzed using statistical techniques such as regression, correlation, ANOVA, factor analysis, and cluster analysis. Software like SPSS or Excel is used.

**Step 5: Present the Findings** *(2 marks)*
A written report and oral presentation is delivered to management. The report should highlight key findings and end with **actionable recommendations**, not just raw numbers.

**Step 6: Make the Marketing Decision** *(3 marks)*
The management team uses the research insights to make strategic decisions. The outcomes are **monitored**, and if new problems arise, the cycle restarts. This feedback loop is what makes MR a continuous, learning-oriented system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> Students often skip the **"feedback loop"** and stop at Step 6. Examiners specifically award **1–2 marks** for mentioning that marketing research is **cyclical**, not linear. Always close your answer with: *"The process is iterative; findings lead to new problems, restarting the cycle."*

---

### **❓ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**"Discuss the various types of Marketing Research. Explain the difference between Marketing Research and Marketing Information System."**

#### **Part (a) — Types of Marketing Research (7 Marks)**

Marketing research can be classified into multiple types based on objective and methodology: *(1 mark for listing)*

1. **Exploratory Research** *(1.5 marks)* — Used to gain initial understanding of a problem. Qualitative methods like focus groups and depth interviews are common. Example: exploring why a new product concept is not resonating.

2. **Descriptive Research** *(1.5 marks)* — Used to describe market characteristics or customer behaviours. Structured surveys, panels, and observational studies are common. Example: measuring customer satisfaction levels.

3. **Causal Research** *(1.5 marks)* — Used to test cause-and-effect relationships. Experiments and test-marketing are common. Example: testing the effect of a 10% price cut on sales.

4. **Predictive Research** *(1 mark)* — Forecasts future market conditions using trend analysis, regression models, and time-series methods.

5. **Qualitative vs Quantitative Research** *(1.5 marks)* — Qualitative explores *why* (motivations), Quantitative measures *how many / how much* (statistical).

#### **Part (b) — Marketing Research vs Marketing Information System (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Marketing Research (MR) | Marketing Information System (MIS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Project-based, one-time | Continuous, routine |
| **Trigger** | Specific decision problem | Scheduled or real-time data flow |
| **Scope** | Narrow, deep dive | Broad, surface-level overview |
| **Data** | Mostly primary | Mostly internal secondary |
| **Cost** | High per project | Lower (automated) |
| **Example** | "Will the new ad campaign work?" | "What were yesterday's sales?" |
| **Time Horizon** | Weeks to months | Real-time / daily |

*(6 marks for table + 1 mark for concluding statement)*

**Conclusion:** MR and MIS are **complementary** — MIS continuously monitors the marketing environment, while MR is invoked when a specific, complex decision demands deeper investigation. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**  
> A common mistake is writing that **"MR is a part of MIS"** — this is **wrong**. They are independent but complementary systems. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this conceptual error. Always state: *"MR and MIS work in parallel, not in hierarchy."*

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Marketing Research** is **systematic, objective, analytical, and purposeful**.
- ✅ **AMA Definition** must be quoted in full for a 14-mark question.
- ✅ **Six-step Process:** Define → Plan → Collect → Analyze → Present → Decide.
- ✅ The process is **iterative** — there is a **feedback loop** from monitoring to problem definition.
- ✅ **Step 3 (Data Collection)** is the **most expensive step**.
- ✅ **Step 1 (Problem Definition)** is the **most critical step** — garbage in, garbage out.
- ✅ **Market Research ≠ Marketing Research** — scope is the key differentiator.
- ✅ **MR ≠ MIS** — MR is project-based; MIS is continuous.
- ✅ **Three main types:** Exploratory, Descriptive, Causal.
- ✅ **Ethical pillars:** Informed consent, privacy, no deception, honest reporting.
- ✅ **Indian examples** (Coca-Cola, Tata Motors, Zomato) fetch extra marks in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Always end a 14-mark answer with a **conclusion** and mention the **feedback loop**.
- ✅ Cite **AMA, ESOMAR/ICC Code, or Malhotra** for academic depth.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT415 - MARKETING RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Service Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Service Marketing** is a specialized branch of marketing that focuses on the marketing of intangible services rather than tangible physical products. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Service Marketing is defined as the process of planning, pricing, promoting, and delivering services to create and exchange value with customers, where the core offering is intangible and does not result in ownership of anything.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Service Marketing is the marketing of economic activities offered by one party to another, most commonly employing time-based performances to bring about desired results in recipients, objects, or other assets for which purchasers are willing to pay.

## 1.2 What is a Service? — The Foundational Concept

A **service** is any act, performance, or experience that one party offers to another, which is essentially intangible and does not result in the ownership of any physical thing. Services are produced and consumed simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The American Marketing Association (AMA) defines services as *"activities, benefits, or satisfactions which are offered for sale, or provided in connection with the sale of goods."*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Service Marketing like a **restaurant dining experience** vs. **buying a packaged meal from a supermarket**:

| Aspect | Tangible Product (Supermarket Meal) | Service (Restaurant Dining) |
|---|---|---|
| **What you get** | A boxed, sealed, pre-made meal | A live cooking, serving, and eating experience |
| **Touch** | You can feel, weigh, and inspect it | You cannot touch the experience before consuming |
| **Ownership** | The meal becomes yours forever | The experience ends when you leave |
| **Quality** | Identical every time (standardized) | Different every visit (depends on chef, mood, crowd) |
| **Storage** | Can be stored in a fridge | Cannot be stored — unused dinner slots are wasted forever |

**The Intuition:** Marketing a service is fundamentally different because you are selling an *experience* that is consumed and vanishes in real-time. You cannot put a haircut on a shelf, you cannot return a flight after take-off, and you cannot touch a software update before installing it.

## 1.4 The Core Distinction: Goods vs. Services

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Insight:** The classification is not binary. Most offerings exist on a **Goods–Services Continuum**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a vending machine (pure good) on one end, and a personal life-coach session (pure service) on the other. A restaurant meal sits closer to the service end, while a sealed packaged snack sits closer to the good end. A car with a maintenance contract sits in the middle.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Exam Tip:** When asked "Distinguish between Goods and Services," always mention that the distinction is *not absolute* but exists on a **continuum**.

## 1.5 The Five Core Characteristics of Services (INTANG, VARI, INSE, PERI, OWNERSHIP)

The most frequently tested topic in KTU exams is the **5 characteristics of services**, summarized by the acronym **I-V-I-P-O** (or the popular **IIPV** model):

1. **Intangibility** — Cannot be touched, tasted, or seen before purchase
2. **Variability (Heterogeneity)** — Quality varies depending on who provides it
3. **Inseparability (Simultaneous Production and Consumption)** — Service is created and consumed at the same time
4. **Perishability** — Unused service capacity is wasted forever (an empty airline seat is lost revenue)
5. **Lack of Ownership** — Customers pay for access, not ownership

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Some textbooks also include **"Customer Participation"** as a 6th characteristic — the customer is often a co-producer of the service.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Characteristics — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Intangibility

| Sub-dimension | Explanation | Real-world Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Intangibility** | Service cannot be touched or felt | Education, medical consultation |
| **Mental Intangibility** | Customer cannot mentally grasp service before purchase | Insurance policy, financial planning |
| **Inability to Inventory** | Services cannot be stored on shelves | An empty hotel room tonight cannot be sold tomorrow |

**Marketing Implication:** Marketers must *tangibilize* the intangible using **physical evidence** (brochures, logos, clean hospital premises, branded uniforms, professional websites).

### 2.1.2 Variability (Heterogeneity)

- Quality of service depends on **who provides it, when, where, and to whom**.
- Two haircuts from the same barber can differ; two surgeries by the same doctor can have different outcomes.
- **Causes of Variability:**
  - Service provider skill, mood, fatigue
  - Customer behavior and demands
  - Time of day, location, environmental conditions

**Marketing Implication:** Invest in **industrialization** of services — standardizing processes (e.g., McDonald's burger-making protocol, hospital SOPs, bank teller scripts).

### 2.1.3 Inseparability

- Production and consumption happen **simultaneously**.
- The customer is present during service delivery.
- **Customer-Provider Interaction** is a critical determinant of service quality.

**Marketing Implication:** Service providers become part of the product. Staff training, appearance, and behavior must be carefully managed.

### 2.1.4 Perishability

- Services are **time-bound** and **cannot be stored, inventoried, or returned**.
- An unsold airline seat for the 6 AM flight is lost forever.
- An unsold 8 PM restaurant table is wasted capacity.

**Marketing Implication:** Demand forecasting, dynamic pricing, and capacity management are critical.

### 2.1.5 Lack of Ownership

- Customers pay for **access, use, or experience**, not ownership.
- A car rental provides temporary use; a movie ticket provides a one-time experience.
- **Value is created during consumption**, not before.

## 2.2 The 7 Ps of Service Marketing (Extended Marketing Mix)

Booms and Bitner (1981) extended the traditional 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) by adding **3 service-specific Ps** to create the **7 Ps framework** — a KTU high-yield topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Favorite Topic:** The 7 Ps of Service Marketing is one of the most asked 14-mark questions. Memorize each "P" with one corporate example.

| P | Element | Core Question Answered | Service-Specific Focus | Real-world Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | **Product** | What is being offered? | Core service + supplementary services | Airtel — mobile connectivity + data plans |
| **P2** | **Price** | What is charged? | Dynamic pricing, yield management | Uber — surge pricing during rain |
| **P3** | **Place** | Where is it delivered? | Multi-site accessibility, distribution | Apollo Hospital chain |
| **P4** | **Promotion** | How is it communicated? | Education, trust-building | LIC Insurance — agent-led selling |
| **P5** | **People** | Who delivers it? | Employee training, attitude, skills | Taj Hotels — hospitality staff training |
| **P6** | **Process** | How is it delivered? | Standardization, automation | Domino's 30-minute delivery process |
| **P7** | **Physical Evidence** | What signals quality? | Tangible cues, ambiance | Apple Store — minimalist, premium interior |

## 2.3 The Service Marketing Triangle (Service Profit Chain Foundation)

The **Service Marketing Triangle** (developed by Philip Kotler and later refined by Heskett, Sasser, and Schlesinger) identifies **three interconnected marketing functions** that work together to deliver service excellence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Theory Note:** The triangle is also the foundation of the **Service Profit Chain**, which links employee satisfaction to customer satisfaction to profitability.

**The Three Vertices:**
1. **Company / Provider** — Sets service standards, trains employees
2. **Employees** — Frontline staff who deliver the service
3. **Customers** — End users who consume the service

**The Three Types of Marketing:**
- **External Marketing** (Company → Customers): Sets expectations through branding, advertising
- **Internal Marketing** (Company → Employees): Trains and motivates staff
- **Interactive Marketing** (Employees ↔ Customers): Service encounters, moment of truth

## 2.4 The GAP Model of Service Quality (SERVQUAL Framework)

Developed by **A. Parasuraman, Valarie Zeithaml, and Leonard Berry (PZB, 1985)**, the **GAP Model** identifies **5 gaps** that cause service quality failures. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU exams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite:** Memorize all 5 gaps in order with one-line definitions. Examiner expects students to draw the diagram AND list the gaps.

### The Five Gaps

| Gap | Name | Definition | Real-world Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gap 1** | Knowledge Gap | Difference between customer expectations and management's perception of those expectations | Hospital management thinks patients want fancy lobby; patients actually want shorter waiting time |
| **Gap 2** | Standards Gap | Difference between management perception and service quality specifications set | Management knows customers want speed but sets unrealistic 2-minute response target |
| **Gap 3** | Delivery Gap | Difference between service specifications and actual service delivered | Staff are overworked, equipment breaks, training is poor |
| **Gap 4** | Communication Gap | Difference between service delivered and what is communicated/promised | Ad promises "5-minute pizza delivery" but reality is 25 minutes |
| **Gap 5** | Expected Service Gap (Total Gap) | Difference between customer expectations and perceived service | The cumulative result of all four provider-side gaps |

### Mathematical Representation of the GAP Model

The total service quality gap experienced by a customer is the cumulative sum of the four provider-side gaps:

$$\text{Gap}_5 = f(\text{Gap}_1, \text{Gap}_2, \text{Gap}_3, \text{Gap}_4)$$

Where:
- $\text{Gap}_1 = \text{Customer Expectations} - \text{Management Perception}$
- $\text{Gap}_2 = \text{Management Perception} - \text{Service Quality Specifications}$
- $\text{Gap}_3 = \text{Service Quality Specifications} - \text{Service Delivery}$
- $\text{Gap}_4 = \text{Service Delivery} - \text{External Communication}$

Therefore:

$$\text{Gap}_5 = \text{Customer Expectations} - \text{Perceived Service}$$

## 2.5 The SERVQUAL Model — Measuring Service Quality

SERVQUAL is the **diagnostic instrument** built on the GAP model. It measures service quality across **5 dimensions** using a **22-item questionnaire** scored on a 7-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree, 7 = Strongly Agree).

### SERVQUAL Formula

$$\text{Service Quality (SQ)} = P_i - E_i$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = Customer's *Perception* of service performance on dimension $i$
- $E_i$ = Customer's *Expectation* of service on dimension $i$
- $i$ = dimension index (1 to 5)
- $SQ > 0$ → Service exceeds expectations (high quality)
- $SQ = 0$ → Service meets expectations (satisfactory)
- $SQ < 0$ → Service falls short (poor quality)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **SERVQUAL** (Expectation − Performance gap model) with **SERVPERF** (Performance-only model by Cronin & Taylor, 1992). SERVPERF simplifies the model by removing expectation measurement.

### The 5 SERVQUAL Dimensions

| Dimension | Focus | Example Statement |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance | "XYZ has modern-looking equipment" |
| **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably | "When XYZ promises to do something by a certain time, it does so" |
| **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | "Employees of XYZ give prompt service" |
| **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust | "Employees of XYZ are consistently courteous" |
| **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention provided to customers | "XYZ has employees who give personal attention" |

### Composite SERVQUAL Score

$$\text{Overall SQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot \left( \overline{P_i} - \overline{E_i} \right)$$

Where $w_i$ is the relative weight of dimension $i$, and $\overline{P_i}$, $\overline{E_i}$ are the mean perception and expectation scores respectively.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Service Quality | $SQ = P - E$ | Core SERVQUAL calculation |
| Overall SERVQUAL | $SQ_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i (\overline{P_i} - \overline{E_i})$ | Weighted service quality score |
| Total Gap | $\text{Gap}_5 = \text{Expectation} - \text{Perception}$ | Customer's perceived service gap |
| Capacity Utilization | $\text{CU}\% = \frac{\text{Actual Sales}}{\text{Maximum Capacity}} \times 100$ | Measures service perishability management |
| Yield Management Index | $YMI = \frac{\text{Revenue Earned}}{\text{Revenue Potential at 100\% Occupancy}}$ | Maximizes revenue per available unit |

## 2.7 Real-world Utility

Service Marketing frameworks are critical in:
- **Healthcare Industry** — Patient experience, hospital service quality
- **Hospitality & Tourism** — Hotel service standards, customer satisfaction
- **Banking & Financial Services** — Customer relationship management
- **Information Technology** — SaaS service delivery, cloud services
- **Education** — Student experience, learning services
- **Telecommunications** — Network services, customer support
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: SERVQUAL Calculation for a Hospital

**Scenario:** A Kerala-based multi-specialty hospital, *Aster Medcity*, wants to measure service quality. A survey of 100 patients yields the following average scores on each of the 5 SERVQUAL dimensions:

| Dimension | Weight ($w_i$) | Mean Expectation ($\overline{E_i}$) | Mean Perception ($\overline{P_i}$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tangibles | 0.15 | 6.2 | 5.8 |
| Reliability | 0.30 | 6.5 | 5.0 |
| Responsiveness | 0.20 | 6.3 | 4.5 |
| Assurance | 0.20 | 6.4 | 5.7 |
| Empathy | 0.15 | 6.0 | 4.8 |

### Step 1: Calculate the Per-Service Gap for Each Dimension

Using the SERVQUAL formula $SQ_i = \overline{P_i} - \overline{E_i}$:

**Tangibles Gap:**

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{Tangibles}} = 5.8 - 6.2 = -0.4$$

This indicates that perception is **0.4 points below** expectation — a minor shortfall.

**Reliability Gap:**

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{Reliability}} = 5.0 - 6.5 = -1.5$$

This is a significant gap — patients expected reliability scores of 6.5 but perceived only 5.0.

**Responsiveness Gap:**

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{Responsiveness}} = 4.5 - 6.3 = -1.8$$

This is the **largest gap** — patients feel that staff are not responsive enough.

**Assurance Gap:**

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{Assurance}} = 5.7 - 6.4 = -0.7$$

A moderate gap.

**Empathy Gap:**

$$\text{Gap}_{\text{Empathy}} = 4.8 - 6.0 = -1.2$$

A substantial gap — patients feel they are not receiving personalized care.

### Step 2: Apply the Weights to Get Weighted Gaps

$$\text{Weighted Gap}_i = w_i \times (\overline{P_i} - \overline{E_i})$$

**Tangibles:**

$$\text{Weighted Gap}_1 = 0.15 \times (-0.4) = -0.06$$

**Reliability:**

$$\text{Weighted Gap}_2 = 0.30 \times (-1.5) = -0.45$$

**Responsiveness:**

$$\text{Weighted Gap}_3 = 0.20 \times (-1.8) = -0.36$$

**Assurance:**

$$\text{Weighted Gap}_4 = 0.20 \times (-0.7) = -0.14$$

**Empathy:**

$$\text{Weighted Gap}_5 = 0.15 \times (-1.2) = -0.18$$

### Step 3: Compute Overall SERVQUAL Score

$$\text{Overall SQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot (\overline{P_i} - \overline{E_i})$$

$$\text{Overall SQ} = (-0.06) + (-0.45) + (-0.36) + (-0.14) + (-0.18)$$

$$\text{Overall SQ} = -1.19$$

### Step 4: Interpret the Result

Since $\text{Overall SQ} = -1.19 < 0$, the service is **failing to meet customer expectations**. The hospital needs urgent action, especially in:
- **Responsiveness** (largest weighted gap of $-0.36$)
- **Reliability** (second-largest gap of $-0.45$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Interpretation Logic:** When a SERVQUAL score is negative, the service is below expectation. The dimension with the largest absolute gap is the **priority area for improvement**. Always state this in your conclusion.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Goods vs. Services

| Dimension | Tangible Goods | Services | KTU Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Physical, touchable | Intangible, experiential | "Goods you can hold, services you remember" |
| **Production** | Made in factory, stored | Produced & consumed simultaneously | "Made-to-order, used-on-the-spot" |
| **Quality** | Standardized (uniform) | Variable (heterogeneous) | "Same brand ≠ same experience" |
| **Storage** | Can be inventoried | Cannot be stored (perishable) | "Empty hotel room = lost revenue forever" |
| **Ownership Transfer** | Yes (you own it) | No (you only access it) | "Buy a car, rent a taxi" |
| **Customer Role** | Passive (purchaser) | Active (co-producer) | "You must be present for a haircut" |
| **Return** | Often returnable | Mostly non-returnable | "Can't return a surgery" |
| **Distribution** | Through supply chain | Direct provider-customer | "Goods travel; service travels to you" |
| **Evaluation** | Before purchase (search) | After consumption (experience/credence) | "Test-drive a car, trust a doctor" |
| **Marketing Mix** | 4 Ps | 7 Ps (adds People, Process, Physical Evidence) | "Services need 3 more Ps" |

## 3.3 Classification of Services by Type

A KTU 2024 frequently-tested sub-topic is the **Lovelock's Classification of Services**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Christopher Lovelock's Service Classification Matrix:** Services can be categorized based on the **tangibility of the service act** (tangible/intangible) and **who receives the service** (people/possessions).

| Receiver ↓ / Tangibility → | Tangible Action | Intangible Action |
|---|---|---|
| **People (Body)** | Health care, passenger transport, salons | Education, broadcasting, theater |
| **People (Mind)** | — | Advertising, consulting, psychotherapy |
| **Possessions** | Freight transport, cleaning, repair | Insurance, banking, legal services |
| **Information/Data** | — | Accounting, data processing, consulting |

## 3.4 Strategies to Overcome Service Characteristics

A frequently asked 14-mark question asks how marketers address the 5 challenges of services:

| Challenge | Strategic Response | Tactical Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Intangibility** | Tangibilize the intangible | Physical evidence, brand imagery, testimonials |
| **Variability** | Industrialize the service | Standardization, SOPs, training, automation |
| **Inseparability** | Manage the moment of truth | Customer co-creation, employee empowerment |
| **Perishability** | Manage demand and supply | Dynamic pricing, reservation systems, yield management |
| **Lack of Ownership** | Create perceived ownership | Membership cards, loyalty programs, branded packaging |

## 3.5 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — A Linkage Tool

QFD (developed in Japan) is often used in service design to translate customer expectations into service specifications. It uses the **House of Quality** matrix.

$$\text{Priority Rating}_i = \sum_{j} w_{ij} \cdot R_{ij}$$

Where:
- $w_{ij}$ = relationship strength between customer requirement $i$ and technical specification $j$
- $R_{ij}$ = importance rating of customer requirement $i$ mapped to specification $j$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Connection:** QFD is not directly in the syllabus but the *concept* of linking customer expectations (the "WHATs") to service operations (the "HOWs") is fundamental to closing **Gap 1** in the SERVQUAL framework.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

