```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[SWITCHERS - Price sensitive buyers]
    L2[SHIFTED LOYALS - Buy 2-3 brands]
    L3[SWITCHING LOYALS - Loyal to 2-3 brands]
    L4[HARD CORE LOYALS - Brand devotees]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme] (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between Brand Identity and Brand Image with one example.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Brand Identity** is how the company *intends* the brand to be perceived; it is **sender-controlled** and includes the brand name, logo, tagline, and value proposition. *Example:* Tata's brand identity — "Leadership with Trust."
- **Brand Image** is how the brand is *actually* perceived by consumers after all interactions; it is **receiver-perceived**. *Example:* Some consumers may view Tata as conservative, while Tata intends to project innovation.
- **Gap** between the two is called the **Brand Reality Gap**, which marketers must close. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme] (3 Marks)
**List any six functions of packaging.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Protection** from physical, chemical, and biological hazards.
2. **Containment** to hold the product.
3. **Convenience** for storage, handling, and use.
4. **Communication** of brand message, ingredients, and usage.
5. **Promotion** at point of sale (the *silent salesman*).
6. **Sustainability** through eco-friendly materials. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — ESE Module-2 Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q. A (a)** Explain **Aaker's Five-Dimension Model of Brand Equity** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the difference between **Brand Awareness** and **Brand Loyalty** in this model. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. A (b)** What is **Packaging**? Discuss the **five layers of packaging** with suitable examples. Explain any **four functions of packaging** in modern marketing. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q. A (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Brand Equity is the value added to a product by its brand name. David Aaker's model identifies five dimensions that create and sustain brand equity.

**Five Dimensions (4 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Brand Awareness** — The ability of consumers to recognize and recall the brand. Operationalized through brand recognition, recall, and dominance (top-of-mind). A high-awareness brand benefits from the "familiarity principle."
2. **Brand Loyalty** — The attachment a customer has to a brand. Measured through retention rate, NPS, and repeat purchase rate. Reduces marketing costs and provides time to respond to competitor moves.
3. **Brand Associations** — The mental linkages consumers have to the brand (attributes, benefits, attitudes). Strong associations = stronger differentiation.
4. **Perceived Quality** — Consumer's perception of overall quality. It is **subjective** and a key driver of purchase decisions and price premium.
5. **Proprietary Brand Assets** — Trademarks, patents, channel relationships, and databases that provide competitive advantage.

**Brand Awareness vs. Brand Loyalty (2 marks — for differentiation):**

| Aspect | Brand Awareness | Brand Loyalty |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Knowing the brand exists | Committing to the brand |
| **Measure** | Recall, recognition | Repeat purchase rate |
| **Stage** | Cognitive (Mind) | Conative (Action) |
| **Risk** | Awareness without action | Loyalty without relevance |

**Conclusion:** Aaker's model shows that strong brands are built on the simultaneous presence of all five dimensions, not just one. A brand with high awareness but no loyalty is fragile. *(7 marks total)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q. A (b)

**Definition of Packaging (1 mark):**
Packaging is the science, art, and technology of enclosing or protecting products for distribution, storage, sale, and use. It is also a marketing tool that communicates the brand promise.

**Five Layers of Packaging (3 marks — 1 each pair):**

1. **Primary Packaging** — Direct contact with product. *Example:* The toothpaste tube, chocolate wrapper, perfume bottle.
2. **Secondary Packaging** — Holds the primary pack. *Example:* The cardboard toothpaste box, carton containing biscuit packets.
3. **Tertiary Packaging** — Bulk handling unit. *Example:* Corrugated box holding 50 toothpaste cartons.
4. **Shipping/Transit Packaging** — Palletization, strapping, containerization for logistics.
5. **Virtual Packaging** — E-commerce unboxing, AR overlays, digital shelf imagery for online retail.

**Four Functions of Packaging (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Protection & Preservation** — Guards against heat, moisture, light, shock, contamination, and tampering. Critical in pharma and food.
2. **Promotion** — Acts as the *silent salesman* on the shelf. The package's design, colour, and typography attract the consumer and trigger impulse purchase.
3. **Convenience** — Easy-tear notches, resealable zippers, and ergonomically designed bottles improve consumer usage experience.
4. **Communication** — Conveys ingredients, nutritional info, MRP, manufacturing date, expiry, brand story, and statutory compliance (FSSAI, ISI marks).

**Conclusion:** In the modern omnichannel world, packaging is no longer a passive container but an **active marketing tool** that integrates brand storytelling, regulatory compliance, sustainability, and consumer delight. *(7 marks total)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — ESE Module-2 Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Q. B (a)** Explain **Keller's Customer-Based Brand Equity (CBBE) Pyramid** in detail. Why is **Brand Resonance** considered the apex of the pyramid? **(7 Marks)**

**Q. B (b)** Explain the concept of **Brand Positioning**. Discuss the **Points of Difference (POD)** and **Points of Parity (POP)** with examples. Write a sample **positioning statement** for a premium ayurvedic shampoo brand. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q. B (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Kevin Lane Keller's CBBE model builds brand equity by **stepping up a six-level pyramid** from the most basic (identity) to the most complex (relationship). The right-side steps represent *rational* route; the left-side represent *emotional* route. Both converge at the apex — Brand Resonance.

**The Six Steps (4 marks — 1 each pair):**

1. **Brand Salience** — Brand identity & category relevance. *Question answered:* Who are you?
2. **Brand Performance** — Functional/utility dimensions. *Question answered:* What are you?
3. **Brand Imagery** — Psychological & social attributes. *Question answered:* What are you like?
4. **Brand Judgments** — Consumer's personal opinions. *Question answered:* What do I think about you?
5. **Brand Feelings** — Emotional responses. *Question answered:* What do I feel about you?
6. **Brand Resonance** — Psychological bond & active loyalty. *Question answered:* What about you and me?

**Why Brand Resonance is the Apex (2 marks):**

- It represents the **deepest psychological connection** between brand and consumer.
- Achieves **active loyalty** (not just repeat purchase but emotional commitment).
- Generates **advocacy** (consumer becomes brand ambassador).
- Creates **community** (brand tribe, e.g., Apple users, Harley owners).
- The most valuable, hardest-to-copy equity — competitor can copy product, not the relationship.

**Conclusion:** A brand that achieves Brand Resonance has built a defensible, long-term moat against competitors. *(7 marks total)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q. B (b)

**Definition of Brand Positioning (1 mark):**
Brand Positioning is the act of designing the brand's offering and image to occupy a **distinctive place** in the target consumer's mind relative to competitors. (Ries & Trout, 1981)

**Points of Difference (POD) (2 marks):**
- PODs are attributes or benefits that consumers **strongly associate with a brand** and view **positively vs. competitors**.
- A POD must be **desirable, deliverable, and differentiating**.
- *Example:* Volvo = Safety; Volvo's POD is the perception of the safest car. Tesla's POD = Innovation in EV technology.

**Points of Parity (POP) (2 marks):**
- POPs are attributes that consumers view as **necessary to be a credible player** in the category.
- Two types: **Category POPs** (must-haves) and **Competitive POPs** (matching competitor claims).
- *Example:* Any toothpaste must claim "fights cavities" (Category POP); any premium smartphone must offer "fast charging" (Competitive POP).

**Sample Positioning Statement (2 marks):**
> *"To young, urban Indian professionals seeking holistic haircare, **KeshVeda** is a premium ayurvedic shampoo that combines 5000-year-old Ayurvedic herbs with modern dermatological science, ensuring chemical-free, visible hair-strengthening results within 3 weeks — because our formula is clinically tested and uses only 100% natural ingredients."*

**Structure followed:** Target + Need + Concept + Key Benefit + Proof.

**Conclusion:** Effective positioning is the foundation of brand equity — without clarity of PODs and POPs, no amount of marketing spend will build a strong brand. *(7 marks total)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Branding & Packaging Questions**
> 1. **Confusing Branding with Logo Design** — Branding ≠ just a logo. It includes strategy, identity, equity, and resonance. Students who only mention logos lose **3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to Diagram Aaker's / Keller's Model** — In a 7-mark question, **mandatory diagram = 1 mark**. Without it, full marks are unattainable. Always draw the pyramid or the 5-dimension wheel.
> 3. **Mixing 5 P's of Packaging with 5 Layers of Packaging** — KTU specifically tests *layers* (Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Shipping, Virtual). The "5 P's" (Product, Price, Place, Promotion, Packaging) is a different concept. Don't confuse them.
> 4. **Skipping Examples** — A 14-mark answer without 2+ real-world brand examples (Amul, Apple, Nike, Tata) feels generic. Examiners reward applied thinking.
> 5. **Ignoring the Indian Context** — In KTU Kerala, an answer that references Kerala-based brands (e.g., *Kerala Ayurveda*, *Nilgiris*, *Manappuram*, *Jos Alukkas*) signals higher-order thinking and earns better marks.
> 6. **One-sided POP/POD** — Always mention *both* categories. Missing either = 1 mark lost.
> 7. **Omitting the Positioning Statement** — In any positioning question, *always* write the **template-driven statement** at the end. It is the differentiator between a 5-mark and a 7-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Branding & Packaging (Module 2)**

### **Branding Essentials**
- Brand = Promise + Identity + Perception
- AMA definition: A name, term, design, or feature that identifies one seller's goods as distinct.
- Brand Elements: Name, Mark, Logo, Trademark, Tagline, Jingle, Personality
- Brand vs. Product: Product is functional; Brand is experiential.
- Brand Equity = Added value beyond functional attributes (Aaker)
- Aaker's 5 dimensions: Awareness, Loyalty, Association, Perceived Quality, Proprietary Assets
- Keller's CBBE: Salience → Performance → Imagery → Judgments → Feelings → **Resonance** (apex)
- Brand Identity (sender) ≠ Brand Image (receiver)
- Brand Positioning: Ries & Trout — occupying a unique, valued place in the consumer's mind
- Positioning uses **POD** (desirable, deliverable, differentiating) + **POP** (category + competitive)
- Brand Mantra: 3-word articulation of the brand's essence (e.g., Nike = "Authentic Athletic Performance")
- Brand Lifecycle: Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline
- Brand Loyalty types: Switchers, Shifted Loyals, Switching Loyals, Hard Core Loyals

### **Packaging Essentials**
- Definition: Container/wrapper for a product; the *silent salesman*
- **5 Layers**: Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Shipping/Transit, Virtual (Digital)
- **6 Functions**: Protection, Containment, Convenience, Communication, Promotion, Sustainability
- **Design Elements**: Shape, Size, Colour, Graphics, Material, Information
- **Decision Process**: Concept → Container → Graphics → Information → Test → Launch → Evaluate
- Modern trends: Sustainable packaging, smart packaging, AR-based virtual packaging
- Indian context: BIS/ISI marks, FSSAI logo, MRP, Veg/Non-veg symbols are mandatory
- Eco-friendly materials: Kraft paper, biodegradable plastics, recycled materials
- Shelf Impact Score: Composite of visibility, readability, and attractiveness

### **Cross-Connectors (Top Exam Cram Points)**
- **Branding** is the mind; **Packaging** is the hand.
- Aaker = 5 dimensions; Keller = 6 steps (both lead to Brand Equity)
- POD ≠ POP; both are *jointly required* for positioning.
- Brand Resonance = highest level; Brand Awareness = first level.
- 5 Layers of Packaging ≠ 5 P's of Marketing.
- Always include a **diagram** in 7-mark theory answers.
- Always include **Indian/Kerala context examples** for 14-mark answers.

### **Must-Memorize 4 Frameworks**
1. ✅ **Aaker's Brand Equity Model** — 5 dimensions
2. ✅ **Keller's CBBE Pyramid** — 6 steps, apex = Resonance
3. ✅ **5 Layers of Packaging** — Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Shipping, Virtual
4. ✅ **POP / POD Framework** — Points of Parity vs. Points of Difference

### **Formulas to Remember (for Numerical/Analytical Questions)**
- $\text{BP} = \dfrac{\text{Buyers}}{\text{Potential}} \times 100$
- $\text{RPR} = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Buyers}}{\text{Total Buyers}} \times 100$
- $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$
- $\text{PSR} = \dfrac{\text{Pkg Cost}}{\text{Selling Price}} \times 100$
- $\text{BEM} = \dfrac{\text{Price Premium}}{\text{Industry Avg Premium}}$

> **End of Module 2 — Branding & Packaging Notes**
> *Stay sharp, stay branded. Good luck for the KTU ESE!* 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pricing Strategies: Pricing Methods and Policies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT415 - MARKETING RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pricing Strategies: Pricing Methods and Policies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pricing (KTU 2024 Definition):** Pricing is the marketing function wherein the firm decides the monetary value at which a product, service, or bundle will be offered to the target market, balancing internal cost structures, customer perceived value, competitor benchmarks, and broader market dynamics to achieve organizational objectives such as profit maximization, market share, or survival.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *Marketing Research*, **pricing strategy** is treated as a **strategic marketing decision** that must be validated by primary or secondary marketing research data. The pricing decision sits at the intersection of the **4 Ps of Marketing** (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) and is directly influenced by **demand elasticity**, **competitive intensity**, and **cost behavior**.

### The Two Pillars of Pricing Decisions
The KTU syllabus specifically bifurcates the topic into:

1. **Pricing Methods** — *the analytical, internal computation* used to arrive at a base price (the "How much does it cost us to make / what will the market bear?" question).
2. **Pricing Policies** — *the strategic, external-facing rules* governing how that price is communicated, adjusted, bundled, and defended over the product life cycle (the "How do we deploy this price across markets, time, and customer segments?" question).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Examiners frequently test the difference between *Methods* (computational) and *Policies* (strategic). A method gives you the **number**; a policy gives you the **rulebook** for using that number.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

**The Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu"**

Imagine you open a restaurant in Kerala called *"Cochin Spice."* Before you print the menu, you face two layers of decisions:

- **Layer 1 — Pricing Methods (Your Kitchen Calculator):** You sit with your chef and ask: *"How much does one plate of *Appam with Stew* cost me in raw materials, rent, and wages? What margin do I need? What are nearby restaurants charging for the same dish?"* This internal calculation gives you the base price — say **₹120**.
- **Layer 2 — Pricing Policies (Your Menu Card & Discount Card):** Now you decide: *Will you charge ₹120 on a Sunday evening when tourists flood the city (peak pricing), or offer a ₹99 "Lunch Thali" combo to attract office workers (psychological + bundle pricing)? Will you give a 10% discount for Zomato orders (channel discount)? Will you let students eat at ₹80 on weekdays (segmented pricing)?*

**The ₹120 is your *method*. The *when, where, to whom, and how* you quote that ₹120 is your *policy*.**

This is exactly the lens KTU examiners want you to apply: cost-plus or value-based numbers are **methods**; skimming, penetration, bundling, and discounts are **policies**.

---

## 1.3 Why Pricing Matters in Marketing Research

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping (Typical):** This topic maps to **CO2 — Analyze the internal and external factors influencing marketing decisions**, and **CO3 — Apply marketing research techniques to strategic decision-making.** Pricing is a *primary data–driven* decision, and Module 2 expects you to understand which research method (conjoint analysis, Van Westendorp PSM, Gabor-Granger) supports each method/policy.

| Pricing Element | Real-World Example | KTU-Relevant Research Tool |
|---|---|---|
| Base Price Determination | MRP on a packet of *Nandini* milk | **Cost accounting + Conjoint Analysis** |
| Customer Willingness to Pay | iPhone launch price | **Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter** |
| Competitive Benchmark | Ola vs. Uber surge pricing | **Competitive pricing audits (secondary data)** |
| Bundle Construction | Amazon Diwali bundles | **Bundle design + A/B Testing** |

---

## 1.4 Pricing in the Marketing Mix (KTU 4 Ps Framework)

| Element | Question it Answers | Pricing's Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Product** | What are we selling? | Pricing reflects **perceived quality** and **positioning** |
| **Price** | What is the monetary value? | The **only P that generates revenue** (others cost money) |
| **Place** | Where is it sold? | Pricing varies by **distribution channel** |
| **Promotion** | How is it communicated? | Price is communicated via **advertising & discounts** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Revenue-Only" Rule:** Of the 4 Ps, **Price is the *only* element that appears in the revenue line of the P&L statement**. All other 3 Ps (Product, Place, Promotion) are *cost centers*. This is why a 1% improvement in price realization often has a **larger impact on net profit** than a 1% increase in volume — a favorite KTU numerical conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pricing Methods (The "How to Calculate" Layer)

Pricing methods, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, are classified into **three primary families** based on the dominant variable in the price calculation formula:

### A. Cost-Based Pricing Methods (Internal-Cost Orientation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** Cost-based pricing sets the price primarily on the basis of the cost of producing, distributing, and selling the product, plus a reasonable rate of profit.

#### Sub-Method 1: Cost-Plus Pricing (Mark-Up Pricing)
- **Logic:** Price = Unit Cost + Mark-up Percentage
- **Formula:** $P = C \times (1 + m)$ where $C$ is unit cost, $m$ is markup % expressed as decimal.
- **Used by:** Manufacturers of standardized goods, FMCG, retail (grocery, garments).
- **Strength:** Simple, ensures cost recovery, fair to buyer and seller.
- **Weakness:** Ignores demand and competition. In a recession, a cost-plus price may be unsellable.
- **KTU Real-World Example:** A textile shop in Thrissur adds 40% markup on a ₹200 saree → sells at ₹280.

#### Sub-Method 2: Break-Even Pricing (Target Profit Pricing Variant)
- **Logic:** Price is set such that **Total Revenue = Total Cost** at a specific sales volume, OR yields a pre-defined target return on investment.
- **Break-even formula:** $BE_{units} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ where $F$ = fixed cost, $V$ = variable cost per unit, $P$ = price.
- **Target return formula:** $P = V + \dfrac{ROI \times Investment}{Unit\ Sales}$
- **Used by:** Industrial goods manufacturers, public sector undertakings (PSUs), B2B firms.

#### Sub-Method 3: Markup Chain Pricing
- **Logic:** Each member of the distribution chain (manufacturer → wholesaler → retailer) adds their own markup.
- **Final Price:** $P_{final} = P_{mfr} \times (1 + m_{wh}) \times (1 + m_{rt})$

### B. Demand-Based Pricing Methods (Customer-Value Orientation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** Demand-based pricing considers the *price elasticity of demand*, *perceived value*, and *willingness to pay* of the target segment.

#### Sub-Method 1: Variable / Dynamic Pricing
- Price fluctuates with **real-time demand** (e.g., Ola-Uber surge, airline tickets, hotel rooms).
- **KTU Discussion Point:** Requires robust **real-time data analytics** — a perfect marketing-research application.

#### Sub-Method 2: Skimming Pricing (used as a *policy*, but the *method* logic is demand-based)
- Sets a *high initial price* to capture consumers with low elasticity (early adopters), then lowers it.
- The *method* of arriving at that high price is by gauging **demand curve sensitivity** at the top of the curve.

#### Sub-Method 3: Penetration Pricing (Method Logic)
- Sets a *low initial price* to maximize volume and lock out competitors.
- The *method* uses **elasticity estimation**: $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{\%\ \Delta Q}{\%\ \Delta P}$ — high elasticity justifies penetration.

#### Sub-Method 4: Psychological Pricing (Odd-Even)
- **Odd pricing:** ₹99 instead of ₹100 (conveys "less than ₹100").
- **Even pricing:** ₹100 instead of ₹99 (conveys "premium/quality").
- **Reference pricing:** Setting a price near a well-known competitor's price.

### C. Competition-Based Pricing Methods (Market-Orientation)

| Sub-Method | Logic | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Going-Rate Pricing** | Match the industry leader's price | Petrol pumps match PSU rates |
| **Sealed-Bid Pricing** | Price is set based on expected competitor bids | Government tenders, e-auctions on GeM |
| **Customary Pricing** | Price set by tradition | Cadbury ₹20 Dairy Milk, Maggi ₹12 |

---

## 2.2 Classification of Pricing Policies (The "How to Deploy" Layer)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A pricing policy is a *consistent, long-term organizational rule* that governs how a firm sets, communicates, and adjusts prices across products, markets, time, and customer segments.

Policies are typically grouped into **four strategic clusters** in the KTU syllabus:

### Cluster 1: New-Product Pricing Policies
1. **Market-Skimming Policy** — High launch price → gradual reductions.
2. **Market-Penetration Policy** — Low launch price → rapid volume capture.

### Cluster 2: Product-Mix Pricing Policies
1. **Product Line Pricing** — Different price points for items in a line (e.g., Samsung Galaxy S, A, M series).
2. **Optional-Product Pricing** — Base + accessories (e.g., car base model + sunroof, alloys).
3. **Captive-Product Pricing** — Main product cheap, consumables expensive (e.g., printer cheap, ink expensive; Gillette razor cheap, blades expensive).
4. **By-Product Pricing** — Sell by-products at market price to reduce main product cost (e.g., sawdust, glycerin from soap).
5. **Product-Bundle Pricing** — Combine products at a discount (e.g., fast-food meal combo).

### Cluster 3: Price-Adjustment Policies
1. **Discount & Allowance Pricing** — Quantity, cash, trade, seasonal discounts.
2. **Segmented Pricing** — Different prices for different customer groups (student, senior citizen, military).
3. **Psychological Pricing** — Odd-even, prestige, reference pricing.
4. **Promotional Pricing** — Loss leaders, special-event pricing, cash rebates.
5. **Geographical Pricing** — FOB, uniform delivered, zone, basing-point.
6. **International Pricing** — Standardized vs. differentiated, dumping, transfer pricing.

### Cluster 4: Price-Change Policies (Initiating & Responding)
1. **Initiating Price Cuts** (defensive against over-capacity, market share loss).
2. **Initiating Price Increases** (cost inflation, over-demand).
3. **Responding to Competitor Price Changes** (match, ignore, reposition).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Methods vs Policies Decision Matrix

| Pricing Element | Category | Key Driver | Best Used When | Risk if Misused |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost-Plus | **Method** | Internal cost | Standardized products, low competition | Volume loss if market won't pay |
| Break-Even | **Method** | Cost + ROI target | B2B, public sector, capital-intensive | Ignores demand reality |
| Skimming | **Policy** | Demand curve, early adopters | Innovative tech, status goods | Invites fast competition |
| Penetration | **Policy** | High elasticity, scale economies | Mass markets, network effects | Margin erosion |
| Bundle | **Policy** | Cross-product demand | Unbalanced demand across SKUs | Cannibalization |
| Captive | **Policy** | After-sale consumption | Durable + consumable combos | Customer lock-in backlash |
| Psychological | **Policy** | Perception heuristics | FMCG, retail | Brand erosion |
| Dynamic | **Policy** | Real-time demand/supply | Travel, e-commerce, utilities | Customer trust loss |
| Sealed-Bid | **Method** | Competitor expectations | B2B tenders, government | Price wars, below-cost wins |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility for Marketing Research Professionals

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Includes This in Marketing Research:** Pricing is *the single most data-tested variable* in a marketing mix. Every pricing method/policy decision is informed by:
> - **Primary research:** Conjoint analysis, Van Westendorp PSM, Gabor-Granger model.
> - **Secondary research:** Competitor price audits, published price indices, scanner data.
> - **Predictive analytics:** Price elasticity modeling, demand forecasting.

A marketing researcher's job in pricing decisions is to **feed each method/policy with the right kind of data** — for instance, *conjoint analysis* informs **product-bundle pricing**, while *van Westendorp PSM* informs **psychological reference pricing**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note (KTU Context):** Since this is a *management/marketing* module, "derivations" are replaced with **structured analytical frameworks, decision-tree walkthroughs, and worked numerical examples** that mirror the kind of 14-mark KTU questions students face. Every cognitive step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: Choosing the Right Pricing Method (Decision Tree Walkthrough)

**Step 1 — Identify the dominant constraint:**
- Is it the **cost structure**? → Go to Branch A (Cost-Based).
- Is it the **demand curve**? → Go to Branch B (Demand-Based).
- Is it the **competitor pricing**? → Go to Branch C (Competition-Based).

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint to derive the price:**
- Branch A: Compute $P = C \times (1+m)$ or $P = V + \dfrac{ROI \times I}{Q}$.
- Branch B: Use elasticity to estimate $\Delta Q$ for any candidate $\Delta P$.
- Branch C: Anchor $P$ to industry leader (going rate) or to estimated bid (sealed bid).

**Step 3 — Overlay a Pricing Policy:**
Once the base price $P$ is computed, choose a *policy* layer:
- New product? → Skim or Penetrate.
- Multiple SKUs? → Bundle or Line price.
- Need to react to a competitor? → Match/Cut/Ignore.

**Step 4 — Validate with marketing research:**
Test the chosen (Method × Policy) combination via:
- Pilot launch in a test market.
- A/B test two price points.
- Conjoint analysis for willingness to pay.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Cost-Plus Pricing (Numerical — KTU Style)

> **Question:** A Kerala-based cashew processor has the following cost structure for 1 kg of premium cashew kernels:
> - Raw cashew nut: ₹400
> - Processing (labour + power): ₹80
> - Packaging: ₹20
> - Transport to distributor: ₹30
> - **Total variable cost per kg: ₹530**
> - Fixed costs (factory rent, admin): ₹5,00,000 per month
> - Expected monthly sales volume: 2,000 kg
> - Desired mark-up: **25%** on total cost per unit.
>
> **Find the selling price per kg using cost-plus pricing.**

### Step-by-Step Solution (Marking-Scheme Style)

**Step 1 — Compute Fixed Cost per Unit:**

$$
FC_{unit} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{2{,}000} = \text{₹ } 250 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Cost per Unit:**

$$
TC_{unit} = VC_{unit} + FC_{unit} = 530 + 250 = \text{₹ } 780
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 25% Mark-up:**

$$
P = TC \times (1 + 0.25) = 780 \times 1.25
$$

$$
P = \text{₹ } 975 \text{ per kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (KTU examiners love this cross-check):**
Profit per unit = 975 − 780 = ₹195.
Total monthly profit = 195 × 2,000 = **₹3,90,000**.
Profit margin % = (195 / 975) × 100 = **20% on sales**.

> **Answer:** The cost-plus selling price is **₹975 per kg**, yielding a 20% profit margin on sales.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Break-Even and Target-Return Pricing (Numerical — KTU Style)

> **Question:** A startup plans to launch a smart IoT energy meter. The per-unit variable cost is ₹1,200. Total fixed costs are ₹24,00,000 per year. The company requires a **20% return on its ₹50,00,000 investment** and expects to sell **10,000 units** per year. Find the break-even price and the target-return price.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Break-Even Price (zero profit):**

$$
BE_{units} = \dfrac{F}{P - V} \implies P_{BE} = V + \dfrac{F}{Q}
$$

$$
P_{BE} = 1{,}200 + \dfrac{24{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 1{,}200 + 240 = \text{₹ } 1{,}440
$$

**Step 2 — Target-Return Price (20% ROI on ₹50 lakh):**

$$
Target\ Profit = 0.20 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
Required\ Contribution = F + Target\ Profit = 24{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 34{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
Contribution\ per\ unit = \dfrac{34{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = \text{₹ } 340
$$

$$
P_{Target} = V + \text{Contribution per unit} = 1{,}200 + 340 = \text{₹ } 1{,}540
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation for KTU exam:**

| Price Point | Value | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{BE}$ | ₹1,440 | Minimum price to avoid loss |
| $P_{Target}$ | ₹1,540 | Price needed for 20% ROI |
| **Margin of safety** | ₹100/unit | Buffer for discounts and policy adjustments |

> **Answer:** Break-even price = **₹1,440**; Target-return price = **₹1,540**. The ₹100 gap is the firm's *policy flexibility* for discounts, bundles, or promotional offers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Demand-Based Pricing Using Price Elasticity (Conceptual)

> **Question:** A beverage company observes that a 10% price increase on its premium drink leads to a 4% drop in quantity demanded. Should it raise the price, and by how much, to maximize revenue, assuming marginal cost is constant?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Price Elasticity of Demand (PED):**

$$
\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{\%\ \Delta Q}{\%\ \Delta P} = \dfrac{-4}{+10} = -0.4
$$

The **absolute value** $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert = 0.4 < 1$ → demand is **inelastic** in this range.

**Step 2 — Revenue Maximization Rule (KTU concept):**
- If $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert > 1$ (elastic) → Lower price to increase revenue.
- If $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert < 1$ (inelastic) → **Raise price to increase revenue.**

**Step 3 — Optimal Price Decision:**
Since demand is inelastic at the current point, the firm should **increase price**. As price rises, elasticity typically increases (demand becomes more elastic) until $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert = 1$ (unit elastic), at which point revenue is maximized.

> **Answer:** Yes, raise the price. The product is currently in the inelastic region, and the firm should keep increasing price until elasticity reaches **unity (|εₚ| = 1)**, which is the revenue-maximizing point.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Framework: Choosing Between Skim vs Penetrate (Decision Matrix)

| Factor | Skim (High Price) | Penetrate (Low Price) |
|---|---|---|
| **Product nature** | High-tech, status, patent-protected | Mass-market, commodity, imitation-prone |
| **Customer segment** | Innovators, early adopters (top of curve) | Early & late majority (volume-driven) |
| **Competitor entry** | Slow, blocked by patents | Fast, must deter via scale |
| **Cost structure** | Low variable cost, high R&D amortization | High fixed cost, low marginal cost |
| **Marketing-research tool** | Conjoint, focus groups for WTP | Van Westendorp PSM, Gabor-Granger |

> **Decision Rule (KTU examiners love this):** *If the **price elasticity of demand** for the new product is **high** (>1), penetration pricing is correct. If the **price elasticity is low** (<1) AND the firm has a **differentiated/unique value proposition**, skimming is correct.*


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — Captive Pricing Diagram:** This is one of the most-tested pricing policies. Note the **two-stage revenue model**: the *base* is a price-elasticity-driven loss leader, while the *consumable* is a price-inelastic recurring annuity. The marketer's research must measure *price elasticity of the base* (high — to set a low entry price) and *price elasticity of the consumable* (low — to set a high refill price).
