# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** As per the KTU 2024 regulations for PECBT415 (Marketing Research), the End Semester Exam (ESE) Module 2 carries questions in the pattern: Part A (3 marks × 2 = 6 marks) and Part B (14 marks × 1 choice-based = 14 marks). Below is a full paper-2 style question bank calibrated to the KTU Board Examiner pattern.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Distinguish between *cost-based pricing* and *value-based pricing* methods. Give one example of each from the FMCG sector.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cost-Based Pricing | Value-Based Pricing |
|---|---|---|
| **Basis** | Internal cost + mark-up | Customer's perceived value / WTP |
| **Formula** | $P = C \times (1+m)$ | Set by primary research, not cost |
| **Data input** | Cost accounting records | Conjoint, PSM, focus groups |
| **Direction** | Inside → Out | Outside → In |
| **FMCG example** | A grocery shop adds 30% to ₹100 rice → ₹130 | Aroma therapy oil sold at ₹800 because customers perceive it as premium wellness |

**Marking tip:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for clear distinction (any 2 of the 5 parameters), 1 mark for the FMCG example.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Question]
**Explain *psychological pricing* with reference to *odd-even pricing* and *reference pricing*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Psychological pricing** is a price-adjustment policy that exploits customer perception biases rather than economic logic. (1 mark)
- **Odd-even pricing:** Charging ₹99 instead of ₹100 makes the product *feel* below ₹100, leveraging the *left-digit effect* (e.g., Notebooks, pens, snacks). (1 mark)
- **Reference pricing:** Setting the price close to a well-known competitor's price (e.g., petrol pumps, toothpaste) so customers evaluate the price using that anchor. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (Dec 2024 onwards):** Candidates answer **ONE** out of two 14-mark questions. Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** [7 marks] 
**Explain the major *cost-based pricing methods* in detail. How does the choice of cost-based method differ between a B2B industrial goods firm and an FMCG retailer? Use a numerical illustration for a B2B firm.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Cost-based pricing is the family of pricing methods that determine the selling price by adding a pre-decided margin or return to the cost of the product. It is the most widely used pricing method in practice due to its simplicity and built-in cost recovery.

**Methods (3 marks):**

1. **Cost-Plus / Mark-Up Pricing:** $P = C \times (1+m)$. The seller adds a fixed percentage to unit cost. Simple and ensures cost recovery. Used in retail and FMCG.
2. **Break-Even Pricing:** $P_{BE} = V + F/Q$. Price is set so that total revenue equals total cost at an expected volume. Used in B2B and capital-intensive industries.
3. **Target-Return Pricing:** $P = V + (ROI \times Investment)/Q$. Builds in a pre-decided return on investment, common in PSUs and B2B firms.
4. **Markup-Chain Pricing:** Each channel member adds a margin; final price compounds. Used in multi-tier distribution.

**Numerical Illustration (2 marks):**
A B2B firm has $V = \text{₹}500$, $F = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$, expected $Q = 2{,}500$ units, target ROI = 20% on investment of ₹30,00,000.

$$
P = 500 + \dfrac{0.20 \times 30{,}00{,}000}{2{,}500} = 500 + 240 = \text{₹ } 740
$$

**Differential Choice (1 mark):**
- **B2B firm** prefers **break-even / target-return** pricing because contracts are long, costs are project-specific, and ROI is contractually negotiated.
- **FMCG retailer** prefers **cost-plus / markup** pricing because inventory turnover is fast, products are standardized, and customers are price-comparison-driven.

---

#### **(b)** [7 marks]
**Discuss *captive-product pricing* and *product-bundle pricing* with suitable examples. What marketing research is required to design each effectively?**

**Model Answer:**

**Captive-Product Pricing (3 marks):**
A pricing *policy* where a base product is priced low (sometimes as a loss leader) to drive volume, while a mandatory consumable / accessory is priced high to generate the actual margin. The customer's "switching cost" creates lock-in.
- **Examples:** Inkjet printers (cheap) + ink cartridges (expensive); Gillette razor (cheap) + blades (expensive); coffee machines (cheap) + coffee pods (expensive).
- **Marketing research required:** (i) Conjoint analysis to measure the *relative price elasticity* of base vs. consumable; (ii) Brand-switching cost surveys; (iii) Recurring-purchase frequency modeling.

**Product-Bundle Pricing (3 marks):**
A pricing *policy* where two or more products are sold together as a single package at a discount, designed to move slow-moving inventory, increase average transaction value, and obscure individual price comparisons.
- **Examples:** McDonald's Happy Meal (burger + fries + drink), Amazon Diwali bundles (phone + earphones + cover), telecom packs (voice + data + SMS).
- **Marketing research required:** (i) Conjoint analysis to determine *bundle utility value*; (ii) Cross-elasticity of demand between bundled items; (iii) A/B testing of bundle composition and discount depth.

**Comparative Insight (1 mark):**
- Captive pricing works on a **temporal** dimension — base now, consumable forever.
- Bundle pricing works on a **transactional** dimension — multiple SKUs, one invoice, one discount.
- Both are **product-mix pricing policies** and both require **primary demand-side research** to design optimally.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a)** [7 marks]
**Explain *market-skimming* and *market-penetration* pricing policies. Under what conditions should a firm choose each? Use real-world examples to support your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

**Market Skimming (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Launching a new product at a *high initial price* and gradually lowering it over time. Targets the *top of the demand curve* — innovators and early adopters with low price elasticity and high willingness to pay.
- **Mechanism:** Revenue is maximized segment by segment as the price is dropped to attract the next customer tier.
- **Conditions favoring skimming:** (i) Product is technologically innovative and patent-protected; (ii) High early-adopter demand; (iii) Low marginal cost (so high price is justified); (iv) Premium image is desired; (v) Slow competitor entry.
- **Examples:** Apple iPhone launch pricing; Sony PlayStation 5 launch; new pharmaceutical drugs under patent.

**Market Penetration (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** Launching at a *low initial price* to win maximum market share quickly, then potentially raising price later once brand loyalty is established.
- **Mechanism:** Works by exploiting *high price elasticity* — a small price = large volume response.
- **Conditions favoring penetration:** (i) Market is highly price-elastic; (ii) Strong economies of scale; (iii) Threat of imminent competition; (iv) Product is a commodity or has many substitutes; (v) Goal is *market share* over *margin*.
- **Examples:** Jio's 2016 launch (free voice + ₹149 data); Xiaomi Redmi phones; Tesla Model 3 in global markets.

**Decision Rule (1 mark):**
*Skim if the product is **differentiated** and demand is **inelastic** at launch. Penetrate if the product is **commodity-like** and demand is **elastic** at launch.*

---

#### **(b)** [7 marks]
**A Kerala-based spice brand wants to enter the international export market. Recommend an *international pricing policy* for the firm. What marketing research would you conduct before setting the final price?**

**Model Answer:**

**Recommended International Pricing Policy (3 marks):**
The firm should adopt a **dual-pronged policy combining *geographical pricing* with *transfer pricing* elements**:
- Use **FOB (Free on Board) pricing** for bulk B2B shipments — buyer bears freight and insurance, simplifying the seller's cost.
- Use **uniform delivered pricing** for premium retail packs sold in metro markets (Dubai, Singapore, US) — include shipping into the displayed price for a clean retail experience.
- Apply **transfer pricing** rules for any intra-group shipments to subsidiaries in target countries.
- Use **price escalation controls** to avoid gray-market re-imports back to India (a key KTU discussion point).

**Marketing Research to Conduct (3 marks):**
1. **Target-market demand study:** Van Westendorp PSM to identify the acceptable price range in each target country.
2. **Competitive audit:** Compare shelf prices of competing Indian and non-Indian spice brands in the target market.
3. **Price elasticity estimation:** Conjoint analysis to understand willingness to pay for "Kerala-origin" and "organic" attributes.
4. **Channel-cost analysis:** Margin expectations of importers, distributors, retailers in the target country.
5. **Regulatory and tariff research:** Anti-dumping duties, import taxes, labelling laws.

**Strategic Conclusion (1 mark):**
The brand should adopt a *differentiated (multi-country) pricing policy* with *psychological sub-pricing* (e.g., $9.99 instead of $10) and a *penetration entry price* in the first 12 months, transitioning to a *skimming* posture once the "premium Kerala" brand is established.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Methods with Policies:** Writing "skimming is a method" loses 2 marks. Skim/Penetrate are **policies**; their *method* of calculation is demand-elasticity-based.
> 2. **Writing only definitions:** A 7-mark sub-question needs **at least one numerical example OR one real-world brand example** to score above 5 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring marketing research link:** PECBT415 is *Marketing Research*. Every pricing question *implicitly* expects you to mention the **research tool** (conjoint, PSM, Gabor-Granger, secondary audit) that supports the decision. Omitting this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 4. **Skipping the elasticity rule:** For demand-based / penetration questions, you **must state** the elasticity rule ($\vert \varepsilon_p \vert > 1$ → penetrate; $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert < 1$ → skim). It is a top-3 favorite KTU question.
> 5. **Forgetting the 4-Ps context:** Always anchor your answer by stating that *Price is the only revenue-generating P*, framing the answer in the broader marketing-mix perspective.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Block — Pin this.**

- ☐ **Pricing has two layers:** **Methods** (compute the number) and **Policies** (deploy the number). Always label which layer you are discussing.
- ☐ **Three Method families:** **Cost-based** (cost-plus, break-even, target ROI, markup chain) | **Demand-based** (elasticity, psychological, dynamic) | **Competition-based** (going rate, sealed bid, customary).
- ☐ **Four Policy clusters:** **New-product** (skim/penetrate) | **Product-mix** (line, optional, captive, by-product, bundle) | **Adjustment** (discount, segmented, psychological, promotional, geographical, international) | **Change-response** (initiate cuts, initiate hikes, respond to rivals).
- ☐ **Elasticity rule (HIGH PRIORITY):** $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert > 1$ (elastic) → **lower** price to grow revenue. $\vert \varepsilon_p \vert < 1$ (inelastic) → **raise** price to grow revenue. Unit elasticity ($\vert \varepsilon_p \vert = 1$) → revenue maximum.
- ☐ **Cost-plus formula:** $P = C \times (1 + m)$. **Break-even formula:** $P_{BE} = V + F/Q$. **Target-return:** $P = V + (ROI \times I)/Q$.
- ☐ **Skim** ↔ inelastic demand, differentiated, innovative, patent-protected. **Penetrate** ↔ elastic demand, commodity, scale-driven, competitor-threatened.
- ☐ **Captive pricing** = base cheap + consumable expensive. **Bundle pricing** = multi-SKU at discount. **Optional pricing** = base + accessories à la carte. **By-product pricing** = monetize waste to lower main product cost.
- ☐ **Psychological pricing** sub-types: **odd** (₹99), **even** (₹100 = premium), **reference** (anchor to competitor), **prestige** (signal high status).
- ☐ **Geographical pricing** sub-types: **FOB** (buyer pays freight), **uniform delivered** (same price everywhere), **zone** (regional bands), **basing-point** (freight from a reference city).
- ☐ **Marketing research tools per pricing decision:** Conjoint for bundle/line; Van Westendorp PSM for psychological/value-based; Gabor-Granger for penetration/skim; Price audits for competition-based; Scanner data for dynamic.
- ☐ **1% price increase ≈ larger profit impact** than 1% volume increase — quote this in any pricing essay for an examiner-pleasing insight.
- ☐ **Decision-rule one-liner to memorize:** *"Cost-based builds a floor, demand-based sets the ceiling, competition-based anchors the market, and policy decides how the firm plays the game in between."*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Price Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT415 - MARKETING RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Price Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Price Determination** is the managerial process of establishing the monetary value at which a product or service will be offered for sale, after systematic analysis of internal cost structures, customer perceived value, competitive intensity, and prevailing market conditions. In marketing research, it represents the **analytical framework** used to arrive at an optimal price point that maximizes organizational objectives (profit, volume, market share, or survival) while remaining acceptable to the target market.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Marketing Research for Price Determination** is treated as a sub-domain of **Module 2: Research Design for Marketing Problems**, where the student is expected to understand how primary and secondary data are deployed to design, test, and finalize the price architecture of an offering.

### Key Terminology (Examiner-Ready Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Price** | The amount of money charged for a product or service, or the sum of values that consumers exchange for the benefits of having or using the product. |
| **Pricing** | The marketing function that involves establishing pricing objectives, identifying the relevant pricing constraints, determining the actual price, and implementing changes. |
| **Price Determination** | The systematic process of arriving at the specific price figure through evaluation of costs, demand, competition, and government/legal regulations. |
| **Price Elasticity of Demand (Ed)** | The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price, mathematically expressed as Ed = (% change in quantity demanded) / (% change in price). |
| **Mark-up Pricing** | Adding a standard percentage to the product's cost to arrive at the selling price. |
| **Break-Even Price** | The price at which total revenue equals total cost (zero profit, zero loss). |
| **Skimming Price** | A high initial price is set to skim the maximum revenue from segments willing to pay a premium. |
| **Penetration Price** | A low initial price is set to penetrate the mass market quickly. |
| **Going-Rate Pricing** | Pricing based primarily on competitors' prices, with less emphasis on cost or demand. |
| **Sealed-Bid Pricing** | Pricing based on expectations of how competitors will price rather than on a strict relation to costs or demand. |
| **Transfer Pricing** | Prices charged when one division of a company sells to another division. |
| **Administered Price** | A price set by managerial policy and held constant for a stated period. |
| **Customary Price** | A price that has been charged for a long time and has become expected by consumers (e.g., newspaper, candy). |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Spice Stall at a Kerala Temple Festival" Model**

Imagine a small entrepreneur named **Arjun** who sets up a banana-fry stall at the **Guruvayur temple festival**. He must decide what to charge per piece. He does not pull a number out of thin air. He:

1. **Looks at his costs** — raw banana, oil, salt, labour, stall rent, electricity.
2. **Feels the demand** — devotees at peak hours will pay more than the casual walkers in the evening.
3. **Glances at the neighbouring stalls** — what are the two other banana-fry sellers charging?
4. **Considers his position** — his stall is near the temple's main entrance, giving him an advantage.
5. **Adjusts for the day** — weekends and festival days can sustain a premium price; weekdays require a discount.

**Price Determination is exactly this thinking process — but formalized, data-driven, and tested.** Arjun's stall is a micro-version of what a marketing researcher does for a national brand using surveys, experiments, and statistical models.

### Why is Price Determination a Marketing Research Problem?

Price is the **only element of the marketing mix (4Ps) that generates revenue**; all others (product, place, promotion) represent costs. Therefore, even a small pricing error can have a disproportionately large effect on profitability. Marketing research provides the **empirical evidence base** to reduce the uncertainty inherent in pricing decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, Price Determination is examined in two distinct strands:
> 1. **Conceptual questions** on factors affecting price and methods of pricing (3-mark level).
> 2. **Applied questions** involving break-even analysis, elasticity computation, or selection of a pricing strategy for a given scenario (14-mark level).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Step Process of Price Determination (KTU High-Yield Framework)

The price determination process is typically modelled as a six-stage sequential decision ladder. Each stage may require primary marketing research or desk-research (secondary data) inputs.

### Step 1 — Establish Pricing Objectives
Pricing must flow from the firm's broader **strategic objectives**. The pricing objective is the goal that a firm tries to achieve through pricing.
Common objectives include:
- **Survival** — short-run price may be set below cost to keep the plant running and workforce intact.
- **Maximum Current Profit** — estimate demand and costs at alternative prices and choose the price that yields the maximum current profit, cash flow, or rate of return.
- **Maximum Market Share** — set the lowest possible price consistent with customer-perceived value to capture the dominant share.
- **Maximum Market Skimming** — set a high initial price to skim the maximum revenue from segments willing to pay the premium.
- **Product-Quality Leadership** — charge a premium to signal superior quality.
- **Other Objectives** — maintain price stability, avoid government scrutiny, support resellers, retain loyalty.

### Step 2 — Determine Demand (Demand Curve & Elasticity)
Every price will lead to a different level of demand, and this relationship is captured by the **demand curve**. Higher prices usually lead to lower demand (with the rare exception of prestige goods — the Veblen effect).

Key research inputs at this stage:
- Price-sensitivity studies (Van Westendorp PSM, Gabor-Granger)
- Conjoint analysis for price-feature trade-offs
- Historical sales data across price points
- Customer surveys on willingness-to-pay (WTP)

### Step 3 — Estimate Costs
Costs set the **floor** for the price. Marketing research rarely changes cost data, but a pricing analyst must understand the cost structure:
- **Fixed Costs (FC):** Costs that do not vary with production or sales level (rent, salaries, depreciation).
- **Variable Costs (VC):** Costs that vary directly with the level of production (raw materials, direct labour, packaging).
- **Total Costs (TC):** TC = FC + VC
- **Average Cost (AC):** AC = TC / Q
- **Marginal Cost (MC):** The additional cost of producing one more unit.

### Step 4 — Analyze Competitors' Costs, Prices, and Offers
The firm must benchmark against the market. Research methods include:
- Mystery shopping
- Competitive price-monitoring panels
- Reverse-engineering competitor cost structures
- Secondary reports (Nielsen, Retail Index)

### Step 5 — Select a Pricing Method
The firm narrows the range set by the demand curve and cost floor using a structured pricing method (see Section 2.3).

### Step 6 — Select the Final Price
The final price considers:
- Psychological pricing (e.g., ₹99, ₹199)
- Channel member expectations
- Company pricing policies
- Competitor reactions
- Legal constraints

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Price Determination

| Internal Factors | External Factors | Marketing-Mix Factors |
|---|---|---|
| Marketing objectives | Nature of the market & demand | Product quality & features |
| Marketing strategy | Competitors' costs, prices, offers | Distribution channel intensity |
| Costs | Economic conditions (recession, inflation) | Promotional intensity |
| Organizational considerations | Government regulations & legal constraints | After-sales service |
| | Buyer behaviour & perception | Brand image |

---

## 2.3 Methods / Approaches of Price Determination (The KTU 6-Model Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Standard Methods of Price Determination (Kotler's 3C framework + 3 additional):**
> 1. **Cost-Based Methods:** Mark-up pricing, Target-return pricing, Break-even pricing.
> 2. **Demand-Based Methods:** Perceived-value pricing, Value pricing.
> 3. **Competition-Based Methods:** Going-rate pricing, Sealed-bid pricing.
> 4. **Market-Oriented (Rate-of-Return) Methods.**
> 5. **Auction-Type Pricing.**
> 6. **Group / Dynamic Pricing.**

The firm rarely picks one method in isolation; the choice is governed by the strategic objective, the market structure, and the available research data.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are tested regularly in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and internal assessments.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Application in Price Determination |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost | $TC = FC + (VC \times Q)$ | Floor of the price range. |
| 2 | Average Cost | $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$ | Used in mark-up computation. |
| 3 | Marginal Cost | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | The supply-side reference for incremental pricing decisions. |
| 4 | Break-Even Volume (units) | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | Volume required to cover all costs at a given price. |
| 5 | Break-Even Price | $P_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{Q} + VC$ | Minimum price to avoid loss at a given volume. |
| 6 | Mark-up Price | $P = \dfrac{C}{1 - r}$ (where $r$ = desired return on sales) | Retail pricing where margin is expressed as % of selling price. |
| 7 | Mark-up on Cost | $P = C \times (1 + m)$ (where $m$ = mark-up % on cost) | Retail pricing where margin is expressed as % of cost. |
| 8 | Target-Return Price | $P = VC + \dfrac{FC + R}{Q}$ (R = target profit) | Used when firms have a stated ROI target. |
| 9 | Price Elasticity of Demand | $E_d = \dfrac{\% \Delta Q}{\% \Delta P}$ | Measures buyer sensitivity to price changes. |
| 10 | Point Elasticity | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \times \dfrac{P}{Q}$ | Used when the change in price is small. |
| 11 | Arc Elasticity | $E_d = \dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_2 + Q_1} \times \dfrac{P_2 + P_1}{P_2 - P_1}$ | Used when the change in price is large. |
| 12 | Marginal Revenue | $MR = P \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}\right)$ | Link between elasticity and revenue optimization. |
| 13 | Optimal Price (Profit Max.) | Profit is max when $MR = MC$ | Foundation of the calculus-based profit-maximizing model. |
| 14 | Price Skimming Indicator | High initial $P$, gradual reduction | Used for innovative tech products (e.g., new iPhone). |
| 15 | Penetration Indicator | Low initial $P$, gradual increase | Used for high-volume FMCG or app-based services. |

### Real-World Engineering / Business Utility of This System
While these formulas are standard in any Managerial Economics or Marketing Research text, their **engineering utility** lies in:
- **Operations management** — Linking production cost curves to pricing decisions for a manufacturing line.
- **Industrial product pricing** — Setting base prices for B2B engineering components.
- **Project valuation** — The break-even formula is identical in structure to the payback-period calculation used in capital budgeting.

---

## 2.5 Pricing Strategies: A KTU-Relevant Classification

| Strategy | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Market-Skimming Pricing** | High initial price, lowered over time. | Innovative electronics, patents. |
| **Market-Penetration Pricing** | Low initial price to gain share fast. | FMCG, OTT subscription launches. |
| **Premium Pricing** | High price signalling high quality. | Luxury cars, premium cosmetics. |
| **Economy Pricing** | Low price with minimal promotion. | Budget airlines, unbranded staples. |
| **Price-Lining** | Limited number of price points for a product line. | Apparel sizes, electronics variants. |
| **Psychological Pricing** | Prices that appear lower (₹999 vs ₹1,000). | Retail, e-commerce. |
| **Promotional Pricing** | Temporary price reductions to spur sales. | Festival sales, end-of-season sales. |
| **Geographical Pricing** | Pricing based on buyer location (FOB, CIF, zone). | Export & import businesses. |
| **Dynamic Pricing** | Prices change based on real-time demand/supply. | Uber, Ola, airline tickets. |
| **International Pricing** | Price differs by country based on market & cost. | Global FMCG brands. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Students often confuse **Skimming** with **Premium** pricing. Skimming implies a *time-based* reduction in price (high → low over the life cycle). Premium implies a *permanently* high price throughout the life cycle. This is a frequent 3-mark trap question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner-Standard Worked Derivations**
> The following derivations are presented in their complete form. Each algebraic transition is explicitly stated so that a student can reproduce the working in the answer script.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Break-Even Price from the Profit Equation

We start with the fundamental profit identity:

$$
\pi = TR - TC
$$

By definition, the **break-even point** is the operating point at which profit $\pi$ equals zero. Substituting:

$$
0 = TR - TC
$$

The total revenue $TR$ is the product of price $P$ and quantity sold $Q$:

$$
TR = P \times Q
$$

Total cost $TC$ has two components — fixed cost $FC$ (independent of $Q$) and variable cost $VC$ per unit (linearly dependent on $Q$):

$$
TC = FC + (VC \times Q)
$$

Substituting the expressions for $TR$ and $TC$ into the break-even equation:

$$
0 = (P \times Q) - \big[FC + (VC \times Q)\big]
$$

Rearranging to isolate the terms on one side:

$$
(P \times Q) = FC + (VC \times Q)
$$

Solving for $Q$ (the break-even **volume** in units):

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}
$$

Now, solving for $P$ (the break-even **price** at a given volume $Q$):

$$
P_{BE} = \frac{FC}{Q} + VC
$$

### Interpretation
- The denominator $(P - VC)$ in the volume equation is the **contribution per unit** — the amount each unit contributes toward covering fixed costs after variable costs are recovered.
- A higher $P$ lowers $Q_{BE}$ (good for the firm), but it may also reduce actual demand, requiring elasticity research to validate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mark-up Pricing Equation

Let the **unit cost** of a product be $C$ and the **mark-up percentage on cost** be $m$ (expressed as a decimal, e.g., $0.25$ for 25%).

The selling price $P$ is defined as cost plus a mark-up:

$$
P = C + (m \times C)
$$

Factorising the right-hand side:

$$
P = C \times (1 + m)
$$

### Useful Re-arrangement: Mark-up on Sales
Often retailers express the mark-up as a percentage of the **selling price** rather than the cost. Let the desired **return on sales** be $r$ (decimal).

Then the mark-up amount is $r \times P$, and the cost equals the selling price minus the mark-up:

$$
C = P - (r \times P) = P \times (1 - r)
$$

Solving for $P$:

$$
P = \frac{C}{1 - r}
$$

**Conversion rule between the two forms:**

$$
r = \frac{m}{1 + m} \quad ; \quad m = \frac{r}{1 - r}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Price Elasticity of Demand (Arc Method)

Consider two observed price-quantity combinations: $(P_1, Q_1)$ and $(P_2, Q_2)$ where $P_2 > P_1$.

The percentage change in $Q$ (using the midpoint formula to avoid bias):

$$
\% \Delta Q = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_2 + Q_1) / 2}
$$

The percentage change in $P$ (using the midpoint formula):

$$
\% \Delta P = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{(P_2 + P_1) / 2}
$$

The arc elasticity is the ratio:

$$
E_d = \frac{\dfrac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_2 + Q_1) / 2}}{\dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{(P_2 + P_1) / 2}}
$$

Simplifying by cancelling the $/2$ terms:

$$
E_d = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_2 + Q_1} \times \frac{P_2 + P_1}{P_2 - P_1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute value convention:** Because price and quantity move in opposite directions for most goods, $E_d$ is usually reported as an absolute value (e.g., $\vert E_d \vert = 1.5$). KTU examiners expect the modulus sign for interpretation but the signed value in the formula.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Interpretation of Elasticity Values

| $\vert E_d \vert$ Value | Classification | Pricing Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert E_d \vert = 0$ | Perfectly inelastic | Firm can change price with no demand change. |
| $0 < \vert E_d \vert < 1$ | Inelastic | Price increase → revenue increase. |
| $\vert E_d \vert = 1$ | Unit elastic | Revenue is constant for small price changes. |
| $1 < \vert E_d \vert < \infty$ | Elastic | Price decrease → revenue increase. |
| $\vert E_d \vert = \infty$ | Perfectly elastic | Any price increase drives demand to zero. |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — A manufacturer of LED bulbs in Kochi**
> A firm produces LED bulbs with the following data:
> - Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹ 2,00,000 per month.
> - Variable Cost (VC) = ₹ 40 per bulb.
> - Current Price (P) = ₹ 100 per bulb.
> - Current Sales (Q) = 5,000 bulbs per month.
>
> The marketing research team has estimated the arc elasticity as $\vert E_d \vert = 1.5$ when price moves between ₹ 100 and ₹ 90.

**Step (a) — Compute the Break-Even Volume at ₹ 100:**

$$
Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{100 - 40} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{60} = 3{,}333.33 \text{ bulbs}
$$

The firm sells 5,000 bulbs, so it is operating above break-even and is profitable.

**Step (b) — Estimate demand at the new price ₹ 90 using elasticity:**

Using the arc elasticity formula (with $P_1 = 100$, $P_2 = 90$):

$$
E_d = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_2 + Q_1} \times \frac{P_2 + P_1}{P_2 - P_1}
$$

Solving for $Q_2$:

$$
1.5 = \frac{Q_2 - 5000}{Q_2 + 5000} \times \frac{90 + 100}{90 - 100}
$$

$$
1.5 = \frac{Q_2 - 5000}{Q_2 + 5000} \times \frac{190}{-10}
$$

$$
1.5 = \frac{Q_2 - 5000}{Q_2 + 5000} \times (-19)
$$

$$
\frac{1.5}{-19} = \frac{Q_2 - 5000}{Q_2 + 5000}
$$

$$
-0.0789 = \frac{Q_2 - 5000}{Q_2 + 5000}
$$

$$
-0.0789(Q_2 + 5000) = Q_2 - 5000
$$

$$
-0.0789 Q_2 - 394.74 = Q_2 - 5000
$$

$$
-0.0789 Q_2 - Q_2 = -5000 + 394.74
$$

$$
-1.0789 Q_2 = -4605.26
$$

$$
Q_2 = \frac{4605.26}{1.0789} \approx 4{,}268.7 \text{ bulbs}
$$

**Step (c) — Compute the new profit at ₹ 90:**

$$
TR_{new} = 90 \times 4268.7 = 3{,}84{,}183
$$

$$
TC_{new} = 2{,}00{,}000 + (40 \times 4268.7) = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}70{,}748 = 3{,}70{,}748
$$

$$
\pi_{new} = 3{,}84{,}183 - 3{,}70{,}748 = 13{,}435
$$

**Compare with the original profit at ₹ 100:**

$$
TR_{old} = 100 \times 5000 = 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
TC_{old} = 2{,}00{,}000 + (40 \times 5000) = 4{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\pi_{old} = 5{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

### Conclusion
Although demand is elastic ($\vert E_d \vert = 1.5$), the *predicted* demand rise (from 5,000 to 4,269 units) is insufficient to compensate for the 10% price cut. **The firm should NOT reduce the price to ₹ 90.** It should retain ₹ 100 or test smaller price cuts (e.g., ₹ 95).

> [!TIP]
> **This is the exact type of integrated problem a KTU 2024 ESE 14-mark question asks.** Practice sketching the break-even chart (a graph with $Q$ on x-axis, $TC$ and $TR$ lines intersecting at the break-even point) for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid diagrams below use alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `objA`) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard against reserved-keyword collisions. All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

---

## 4.1 Master Flow Diagram: Six-Step Price Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STAGE 1 ESTABLISH PRICING OBJECTIVES"] --> node2["STAGE 2 DETERMINE DEMAND CURVE AND ELASTICITY"]
    node2 --> node3["STAGE 3 ESTIMATE COSTS FIXED VARIABLE MARGINAL"]
    node3 --> node4["STAGE 4 ANALYZE COMPETITORS COSTS PRICES OFFERS"]
    node4 --> node5["STAGE 5 SELECT A PRICING METHOD"]
    node5 --> node6["STAGE 6 SELECT FINAL PRICE WITH PSYCHOLOGICAL AND LEGAL ADJUSTMENTS"]
    node6 --> node7["IMPLEMENT AND MONITOR"]

    objA["PROFIT MAXIMIZATION"] -.-> node1
    objB["MARKET SHARE"] -.-> node1
    objC["SURVIVAL"] -.-> node1
    objD["QUALITY LEADERSHIP"] -.-> node1
```

**Reading the diagram:** The bold black arrows represent the mandatory linear sequence. The dotted arrows feeding into Stage 1 are the *pricing objectives* that act as upstream inputs to the entire process.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Three Major Pricing Method Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    root["SELECT A PRICING METHOD"] --> fam1["COST BASED FAMILY"]
    root --> fam2["DEMAND BASED FAMILY"]
    root --> fam3["COMPETITION BASED FAMILY"]

    fam1 --> m1["MARKUP PRICING"]
    fam1 --> m2["TARGET RETURN PRICING"]
    fam1 --> m3["BREAK EVEN PRICING"]

    fam2 --> m4["PERCEIVED VALUE PRICING"]
    fam2 --> m5["VALUE PRICING"]
    fam2 --> m6["PSYCHOLOGICAL PRICING"]

    fam3 --> m7["GOING RATE PRICING"]
    fam3 --> m8["SEALED BID PRICING"]
    fam3 --> m9["AUCTION TYPE PRICING"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A firm chooses one or a hybrid combination from three families. The choice is driven by the data the marketing-research function can supply — for example, perceived-value pricing requires extensive primary research (conjoint, focus groups), while going-rate pricing can rely on secondary competitor-tracking data.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of Marketing Research in Pricing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    obj["RESEARCH OBJECTIVE TO DETERMINE OPTIMAL PRICE"] --> des["RESEARCH DESIGN EXPLORATORY DESCRIPTIVE CAUSAL"]
    des --> dat["DATA COLLECTION PRIMARY SURVEYS EXPERIMENTS AND SECONDARY DATA"]
    dat --> ana["DATA ANALYSIS DEMAND CURVE ELASTICITY MODELS CONJOINT ANALYSIS"]
    ana --> ins["INSIGHTS PRICE SENSITIVITY WILLINGNESS TO PAY OPTIMAL PRICE BAND"]
    ins --> dec["MANAGERIAL DECISION FINAL PRICE LAUNCH MONITORING PLAN"]
    dec --> fb["POST LAUNCH FEEDBACK LOOP"]

    fb -.-> des
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the closed-loop research process. The post-launch feedback loop re-injects field data back into the design stage, enabling continuous price optimization — a key NEP 2020 outcome of lifelong learning and iterative improvement.

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Which Pricing Strategy to Choose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["NEW PRODUCT LAUNCH"] --> q1["IS THE PRODUCT TECHNICALLY INNOVATIVE"]
    q1 -- YES --> strat1["SKIMMING PRICING"]
    q1 -- NO --> q2["IS MARKET HIGHLY PRICE SENSITIVE"]
    q2 -- YES --> strat2["PENETRATION PRICING"]
    q2 -- NO --> q3["IS COMPETITION INTENSE"]
    q3 -- YES --> strat3["GOING RATE PRICING"]
    q3 -- NO --> q4["DOES THE BRAND SIGNAL LUXURY"]
    q4 -- YES --> strat4["PREMIUM PRICING"]
    q4 -- NO --> strat5["COMPETITIVE OR ECONOMY PRICING"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each diamond is a decision node, each rectangle is a strategy outcome. This is a high-yield diagram for the **"Discuss the pricing strategy suitable for a given product"** type of 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pattern Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A: 3-mark short-answer questions, typically one or two from this module.
> - Part B: 14-mark questions with internal choice; Module 2 generally contributes one full 14-mark question.
> - Bloom's levels tested: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyse, Evaluate.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECBT415]**
**Define price determination. List any two methods of price determination.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Price determination is the managerial process of arriving at a specific monetary value at which a product or service will be offered to the target market, after a systematic analysis of costs, demand, competition, and legal constraints. It is the analytical decision point where the firm reconciles the cost floor (set by the cost structure) with the demand ceiling (set by perceived value) using a structured pricing method.

**Two methods of price determination:**
1. **Cost-based pricing** (e.g., mark-up pricing) — Price = Unit cost + a fixed percentage margin.
2. **Competition-based pricing** (e.g., going-rate pricing) — Price is set close to the prevailing competitor price in the market.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Tip:** Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for any two methods, ½ mark for a one-line description. Avoid using generic phrases like "pricing is important" without naming a method.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECBT415]**
**Distinguish between market skimming pricing and market penetration pricing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Market Skimming | Market Penetration |
|---|---|---|
| Initial price | High | Low |
| Objective | Capture revenue from innovators willing to pay premium | Capture mass market share quickly |
| Life-cycle trajectory | Price is gradually lowered | Price is gradually raised |
| Typical use | Innovative tech, patented products | FMCG, OTT subscriptions |
| Time horizon | Short-term revenue max | Long-term volume max |

A clean comparative table earns full marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently state that skimming is a "permanent high price" and penetration is a "permanent low price." This is incorrect. Both are *time-based* strategies with opposite trajectories. Marks are deducted for failing to note the temporal dimension.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern, PECBT415]**

**"A start-up company in Kerala is planning to launch a new range of organic green tea targeting urban health-conscious millennials. As a marketing research consultant, advise the firm on a suitable pricing strategy, the factors to consider, and the research methods that should be deployed to finalize the price."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Recommend a suitable pricing strategy and justify your choice using the factors that affect pricing decisions for this product.**

**Model Answer:**

**Recommended Strategy: Market Penetration Pricing followed by a gradual transition to Value-Based Pricing.**

**Justification — Factors Considered:**

1. **Nature of the market:** The target segment is urban, price-sensitive millennials accustomed to comparing prices on e-commerce platforms (Amazon, Flipkart, BigBasket). A low entry price will rapidly build trial and word-of-mouth.
2. **Stage in the product life cycle:** As a market entry, the product is in the introduction stage where awareness is low; penetration pricing drives trial.
3. **Competitor pricing:** Established players (e.g., Organic Tattva, Lipton Green) have set reference points. A penetration price slightly below the going rate secures an early mover advantage.
4. **Cost structure:** The firm must verify that the variable cost per pack is low enough to support a discounted introductory price without triggering a loss.
5. **Marketing-mix consistency:** The promotional plan already emphasizes affordability, so the price must align with that positioning.
6. **Long-term objective:** The firm targets *market share* and *customer acquisition* in year one; revenue maximization is a year-three objective. This makes penetration aligned with the objective.
7. **Legal/distribution factors:** Packed tea is regulated under FSSAI; pricing must comply with MRP labelling norms.

> [!TIP]
> **Marker Note (7 marks):** Award 1 mark for naming the strategy, 4 marks for factor-by-factor analysis (½ mark each, minimum four factors), and 2 marks for the integrated justification connecting factors to the strategic objective.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Outline the marketing research methods that should be used to finalize the introductory price, and explain how each method contributes to the price decision.**

**Model Answer:**

| Research Method | Purpose / Contribution to Pricing |
|---|---|
| **Exploratory focus groups (n = 6 groups × 8 millennials)** | Discover consumer language on price, perceived value, and reference points. |
| **Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter (PSM)** | Identifies four price thresholds: too cheap, cheap, expensive, too expensive. The optimal price is between "cheap" and "expensive." |
| **Gabor-Granger Method** | Asks respondents their purchase likelihood at a range of prices, building a demand curve and pinpointing the revenue-maximizing price. |
| **Conjoint Analysis** | Quantifies the trade-off between price and other attributes (e.g., organic certification, flavour, packaging size) to derive the *part-worth* of price. |
| **Brand-Price Trade-Off (BPTO)** | Identifies at what price the brand loses its premium positioning or, conversely, gains credibility. |
| **Test marketing / Simulated test market** | A real-world test of the recommended price in a limited geography (e.g., a single Kerala district) before national rollout. |
| **Competitor price tracking panel** | Continuous secondary data on competitor prices to enable dynamic adjustment. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The combination of Van Westendorp, Gabor-Granger, and Conjoint gives the firm a defensible empirical basis to set the introductory price. Test marketing validates the choice in a real-market setting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part b):** Students commonly list *one* research method (e.g., "we will conduct a survey") and stop. To earn full 7 marks, you must (i) name at least three methods, (ii) explain the data each method generates, and (iii) link each method's output to a specific pricing decision. Generic statements like "surveys help" are not acceptable.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PECBT415]**

**"A firm has the following data for a product line: Fixed Cost = ₹ 5,00,000; Variable Cost = ₹ 200 per unit; Current Price = ₹ 500; Current Sales = 2,000 units. The marketing research team estimates the price elasticity of demand as 1.8 in the price range ₹ 400–₹ 500. Should the firm increase the price to ₹ 550? Justify your answer with full calculations."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the current profit, the break-even price at the current volume, and the new quantity demanded at ₹ 550 using the arc elasticity formula.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Current Profit (1 mark):**

$$
TC = 5{,}00{,}000 + (200 \times 2000) = 5{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 9{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
TR = 500 \times 2000 = 10{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\pi = 10{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Break-Even Price at current volume (2 marks):**

$$
P_{BE} = \frac{FC}{Q} + VC = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2000} + 200 = 250 + 200 = 450
$$

The firm is currently selling at ₹ 500, which is ₹ 50 above the break-even price. This is consistent with the positive profit computed.

**Step 3 — New Quantity at ₹ 550 using arc elasticity (4 marks):**

Let $P_1 = 500$, $P_2 = 550$, $Q_1 = 2000$, $E_d = 1.8$.

$$
E_d = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{Q_2 + Q_1} \times \frac{P_2 + P_1}{P_2 - P_1}
$$

$$
1.8 = \frac{Q_2 - 2000}{Q_2 + 2000} \times \frac{550 + 500}{550 - 500}
$$

$$
1.8 = \frac{Q_2 - 2000}{Q_2 + 2000} \times \frac{1050}{50}
$$

$$
1.8 = \frac{Q_2 - 2000}{Q_2 + 2000} \times 21
$$

$$
\frac{1.8}{21} = \frac{Q_2 - 2000}{Q_2 + 2000}
$$

$$
0.0857 = \frac{Q_2 - 2000}{Q_2 + 2000}
$$

$$
0.0857(Q_2 + 2000) = Q_2 - 2000
$$

$$
0.0857 Q_2 + 171.4 = Q_2 - 2000
$$

$$
171.4 + 2000 = Q_2 - 0.0857 Q_2
$$

$$
2171.4 = 0.9143 Q_2
$$

$$
Q_2 = \frac{2171.4}{0.9143} \approx 2375 \text{ units}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Check:** The computed result $Q_2 \approx 2375$ implies that the elasticity in the ₹ 400–₹ 500 range *cannot* be directly extrapolated to a price *increase* to ₹ 550. The data point shows that a price *decrease* would yield higher demand; a price *increase* to ₹ 550 would actually **reduce** demand, not raise it. The model is being applied beyond its validated range. This nuance is expected in a top-band answer.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Re-evaluate using the correct sign convention. Should the firm raise the price to ₹ 550? Compute the new profit and provide a managerial recommendation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the correct sign convention (1 mark):**
For a *price increase*, the expected change in quantity is *negative*. Using the elasticity formula with the correct sign:

$$
E_d = 1.8 \rightarrow \text{for a 10% price rise}, \% \Delta Q = -18\%
$$

$$
Q_2 = 2000 \times (1 - 0.18) = 2000 \times 0.82 = 1640 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Recompute the new profit at ₹ 550 with Q₂ = 1,640 (2 marks):**

$$
TR_{new} = 550 \times 1640 = 9{,}02{,}000
$$

$$
TC_{new} = 5{,}00{,}000 + (200 \times 1640) = 5{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}28{,}000 = 8{,}28{,}000
$$

$$
\pi_{new} = 9{,}02{,}000 - 8{,}28{,}000 = 74{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the original profit (1 mark):**
Original profit = ₹ 1,00,000. New profit = ₹ 74,000. The new profit is **lower** by ₹ 26,000.

**Step 4 — Managerial Recommendation (3 marks):**
1. The firm should **NOT** raise the price to ₹ 550. The demand is elastic in this segment, so a price hike causes a proportionally larger drop in demand and erodes profit.
2. The firm should consider a **modest price reduction** (e.g., to ₹ 450) where elasticity of 1.8 implies a 9% volume increase (to 2,180 units), leading to higher revenue and profit.
3. Long term, the firm should pursue **perceived-value pricing** reinforced by brand-building to reduce the elasticity coefficient (i.e., make demand less price-sensitive).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Full 14 marks):**
> - **Sign error on elasticity** is the most common mistake in this problem. Always use the sign convention consistently.
> - Students who skip the cross-check of *whether the elasticity range is valid for a price increase* lose 2 marks.
> - A *managerial recommendation* that is not supported by the numbers earns partial credit only. Recommendation must quantitatively cite the profit change.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this list on your study wall. Each bullet is a potential 1–3 mark KTU question.**

- **Price** is the only P that generates revenue; the other 3Ps are cost centres.
- **Price Determination** is a six-step process: Objectives → Demand → Costs → Competitors → Method → Final Price.
- **Pricing objectives** include survival, profit max, market share, market skimming, and quality leadership.
- **Cost-based pricing** uses the cost floor: $P = C \times (1 + m)$ or $P = C / (1 - r)$.
- **Break-even price**: $P_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{Q} + VC$.
- **Break-even volume**: $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$.
- **Demand-based pricing** uses the demand ceiling — perceived value, WTP, psychological anchors.
- **Competition-based pricing**: Going rate, sealed bid, auction.
- **Mark-up vs. Margin:** Mark-up is on cost; margin is on selling price. $r = m / (1 + m)$.
- **Arc elasticity** uses the midpoint formula; **point elasticity** uses the derivative.
- **Inelastic demand** ($|E_d| < 1$) → price hike increases revenue.
- **Elastic demand** ($|E_d| > 1$) → price cut increases revenue.
- **Unit elastic** ($|E_d| = 1$) → revenue is constant for small changes.
- **Skimming vs. Penetration:** Skimming = high → low over time; Penetration = low → high over time.
- **Premium vs. Skimming:** Premium = *permanently* high; Skimming = *temporarily* high.
- **Psychological pricing** uses anchors like ₹ 99, ₹ 199, ₹ 999.
- **Geographical pricing** considers FOB, CIF, zone, and base-point systems.
- **Dynamic pricing** is real-time, common in ride-hailing, airlines, and hotel aggregators.
- **Value pricing** wins the long game by aligning price with the customer's perceived benefits, not the firm's cost.
- **Research methods for price determination:** Van Westendorp PSM, Gabor-Granger, Conjoint, BPTO, test marketing, competitor tracking.
- **Profit-maximization rule:** $MR = MC$, which links to $MR = P(1 - 1/|E_d|)$.
- **The break-even chart** is a straight-line graphical tool; intersection of $TR$ and $TC$ gives the break-even point.
- **Always sign your elasticity** correctly: a price *increase* must lead to a *decrease* in $Q$ for ordinary goods.
- **Customary prices** (e.g., newspaper) are sticky and psychologically anchored; changing them requires careful research.
- **KTU 14-mark format** always demands a managerial recommendation tied to numbers; never end with a generic "the firm should study the market."

> **End of Module 2 — Price Determination Notes (PECBT415, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT415 - MARKETING RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Marketing Research: Introduction, Types of Market Research, Scope, Objectives, and Limitations; Marketing Research Techniques; Survey Questionnaire Design and Drafting; Pricing Research; Media Research; Qualitative Research

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Marketing Research – Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Marketing Research is the systematic, objective, and exhaustive process of designing, gathering, analyzing, and reporting information relevant to identifying and solving marketing problems or opportunities. It applies the scientific method to managerial decision-making regarding the 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) of the marketing mix.*

In KTU 2024 terminology, marketing research is positioned as the **"decision-supporting nervous system"** of any modern enterprise. It is the bridge between the marketer and the marketplace, transforming unstructured intuition into structured, evidence-based intelligence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms Used in KTU Paper Patterns:**
> - MR (Marketing Research)
> - Marketing Intelligence
> - Consumer Insight Generation

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Doctor for a Business"

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor:
1. Asks questions (**Survey/Questionnaire**)
2. Runs blood tests (**Data Collection & Analysis**)
3. Reviews X-rays and MRIs (**Qualitative & Quantitative Research**)
4. Diagnoses the disease (**Problem Identification**)
5. Prescribes treatment (**Marketing Decision Making**)

Similarly, **Marketing Research is the medical check-up for a business**. Without it, the manager is "prescribing in the dark," which usually results in either a dying product (loss) or an over-investment in the wrong direction (waste of capital).

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - *Intuition + No Research = Gambling*
> - *Intuition + Marketing Research = Strategic Winning*

---

## 1.3 Three Pillars of the Marketing Research Definition (Examiner's Favorite)

Any KTU answer on "Define Marketing Research" must highlight these three pillars for full marks:

| Pillar | Meaning | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Systematic** | Follows a structured step-by-step process (not random guesswork) | Six-step research process |
| **Objective** | Free from personal bias; data-driven | Neutrality and validity |
| **Exhaustive** | Covers all relevant variables, not just one aspect | Sample size and depth of analysis |

---

## 1.4 Why Marketing Research is Indispensable (KTU 2024 Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Point:**
> In the era of **Big Data, AI-driven personalization, and hyper-competition**, marketing research has shifted from being a "luxury" (used only by MNCs) to a "necessity" (used even by kirana stores through Google My Business analytics).

Key drivers:
- **Customer Empowerment:** Customers now compare products across geographies in seconds.
- **Market Saturation:** Most product categories are mature; growth comes from insights, not inventions.
- **Risk Reduction:** A new product launch failure rate in India is ~70–80%. Research can cut this dramatically.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** For example, pharmaceutical and FMCG advertising claims must be substantiated.

---

## 1.5 Standard Marketing Research Process (KTU Flowchart Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The American Marketing Association (AMA) defines a **Six-Step Research Process**. KTU examiners expect this exact sequence:

1. **Define the Problem & Research Objectives**
2. **Develop the Research Plan / Design**
3. **Collect the Information / Data**
4. **Analyze the Information**
5. **Present the Findings**
6. **Make the Decision / Take Action**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Types of Market Research (Comprehensive Classification)

Marketing research is classified along **four major dimensions** as per KTU 2024 module structure:

### Dimension 1: By Purpose / Objective

| Type | Definition | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Exploratory Research** | Preliminary investigation to gain insights and define the problem | When problem is vaguely defined |
| **Descriptive Research** | Describes market characteristics or functions (who, what, when, where, how) | To profile customers, market share |
| **Causal Research (Experimental)** | Tests cause-and-effect relationships | "Will a 10% price cut increase sales by X%?" |
| **Predictive Research** | Forecasts future trends and outcomes | Demand forecasting, sales projection |

### Dimension 2: By Data Collection Method

| Type | Data Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Research** | First-hand, collected for the current problem | Surveys, interviews, experiments |
| **Secondary Research** | Already existing data (internal & external) | Government reports, census, published journals |

### Dimension 3: By Approach / Nature

| Type | Philosophy | Sample Size |
|---|---|---|
| **Qualitative Research** | Exploratory, subjective, small samples | 10–30 respondents |
| **Quantitative Research** | Structured, statistical, large samples | 200+ respondents |

### Dimension 4: By Frequency

- **Continuous Research** – ongoing (e.g., brand tracking)
- **Ad-hoc Research** – one-time, for specific decisions
- **Periodic Research** – at regular intervals (e.g., annual customer satisfaction survey)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic: "E D C P"** for the four purposes:
> **E**xploratory → **D**escriptive → **C**ausal → **P**redictive

---

## 2.2 Scope of Marketing Research (KTU 2024 Detailed Scope)

The scope is **vast** and covers all functional areas of marketing:

### A. Product Research
- Concept testing
- Brand name testing
- Packaging research
- Product modification and line extension studies
- Test marketing

### B. Pricing Research
- Price elasticity measurement
- Price sensitivity (Van Westendorp model)
- Competitor pricing analysis
- Value-based pricing studies

### C. Promotion Research
- Advertising effectiveness (pre & post)
- Copy testing
- Media research (reach, frequency, GRPs)
- Sales promotion evaluation
- Public relations effectiveness

### D. Place / Distribution Research
- Channel performance
- Retail audit
- Logistics and supply chain
- Coverage and distribution intensity

### E. Consumer Research
- Buying behavior
- Brand loyalty and switching
- Customer satisfaction (CSAT, NPS)
- Lifestyle and psychographics

---

## 2.3 Objectives of Marketing Research (KTU Examiner's Blueprint)

The principal objectives are grouped into **four functional clusters**:

1. **Identification of Marketing Problems & Opportunities**
   - Spotting unmet consumer needs
   - Identifying new segments

2. **Generation, Refinement, and Evaluation of Marketing Actions**
   - Testing alternative marketing mixes
   - Comparing strategic options

3. **Monitoring Marketing Performance**
   - Tracking brand health
   - Measuring advertising ROI

4. **Improving Understanding of Marketing as a Process**
   - Building consumer behavior models
   - Forecasting demand accurately

---

## 2.4 Limitations of Marketing Research

> [!WARNING]
> KTU 2024 explicitly tests this section. Students often write 2–3 points; aim for **at least 6** to fetch full 14 marks.

| # | Limitation | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-Consuming** | A full-scale study may take 3–6 months |
| 2 | **Costly** | Primary research with pan-India sample may cost ₹5–50 lakhs |
| 3 | **Biased Results** | Respondent bias, interviewer bias, sponsor bias |
| 4 | **Limited Scope** | Cannot capture the entire population (sampling error) |
| 5 | **Dynamic Markets** | Data may become obsolete quickly in fast-changing markets |
| 6 | **Subjectivity in Qualitative Research** | Interpretation varies by researcher |
| 7 | **Information Overload** | Too much data without proper analysis = paralysis |
| 8 | **Forecasting Inaccuracy** | Especially in disruptive technology markets |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a Management course, the "formulas" are **decision-making frameworks, statistical tests, and model equations** used in MR.

| # | Framework / Equation | Purpose | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Size (Cochran's Formula):** $n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{e^2}$ | Determining minimum sample size for proportion | Survey design |
| 2 | **Adjusted Sample Size:** $n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{(n_0 - 1)}{N}}$ | Finite population correction | Census data |
| 3 | **Likert Scale Scoring:** $\bar{X} = \frac{\sum f_i x_i}{N}$ | Mean attitude score | Questionnaire analysis |
| 4 | **Price Elasticity of Demand:** $E_p = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$ | Sensitivity of demand to price | Pricing research |
| 5 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS):** $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | Customer loyalty metric | Customer satisfaction |
| 6 | **Customer Satisfaction Index:** $\text{CSI} = \frac{\sum w_i \bar{x}_i}{\sum w_i} \times 100$ | Composite satisfaction measure | Service MR |
| 7 | **GRPs (Gross Rating Points):** $\text{GRP} = \text{Reach} \times \text{Frequency}$ | Media planning metric | Media research |
| 8 | **Cost Per Rating Point (CPRP):** $\text{CPRP} = \frac{\text{Media Cost}}{\text{GRPs}}$ | Media efficiency | Media research |
| 9 | **Barth Index / Readability:** $\text{RE} = 1.599 \cdot \text{ASL} - 1.015 \cdot \text{ASW} - 31.517$ | Question difficulty level | Questionnaire design |
| 10 | **Response Rate:** $\text{RR} = \frac{\text{Completed Responses}}{\text{Total Distributed}} \times 100$ | Survey success measure | Survey research |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Definitions for Formulas:**
> - $Z$ = Z-score (1.96 for 95% confidence)
> - $p$ = estimated proportion (usually 0.5 for max sample)
> - $q = 1 - p$
> - $e$ = acceptable margin of error
> - $N$ = population size
> - $f_i$ = frequency of each response
> - $x_i$ = Likert score value (1–5)
> - $\%P$ = % Promoters, $\%D$ = % Detractors

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Marketing Research

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering/B.Tech context (KTU 2024 NEP alignment):**
> - **Tech Product Managers** (especially in CS/IT) require MR before launching any app feature.
> - **B.Tech startups** use MR for product-market fit validation.
> - **Engineers entering MBA/management roles** will directly apply these frameworks.
> - The **interdisciplinary** nature of B.Tech (especially under NEP 2020's multidisciplinary vision) makes MR essential even for non-management students.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Marketing Research Techniques – Detailed Analysis

There are **three primary categories** of marketing research techniques:

### A. Survey Research Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** A structured, quantitative approach involving the systematic collection of data from a sample using questionnaires/interviews.

**Types of Surveys:**

1. **Cross-Sectional Survey** – Data collected once from a sample at a single point in time.
   - *Example:* A single customer satisfaction study.
2. **Longitudinal Survey** – Data collected repeatedly from the same sample over time.
   - *Example:* Brand tracking study every quarter.
3. **Mail Surveys** – Postal questionnaire.
4. **Telephone Surveys** – CATI (Computer Assisted Telephone Interviewing).
5. **Online / E-Surveys** – Google Forms, SurveyMonkey, Qualtrics.
6. **Personal (Face-to-Face) Surveys** – Door-to-door, mall intercepts.

**Advantages of Survey Technique:**
- Highly structured
- Easy to administer to large samples
- Quantifiable results
- Results can be generalized (if sample is representative)

**Limitations of Survey Technique:**
- Superficial (depth missing)
- Respondent fatigue
- Low response rates (mail surveys: 5–10%)
- Social desirability bias

---

### B. Observational Research Technique

Data collected by **watching behavior** rather than asking questions.

**Types:**
1. **Naturalistic Observation** – Subjects unaware (e.g., CCTV in retail stores)
2. **Contrived Observation** – Artificial setting (e.g., eye-tracking lab)
3. **Direct Observation** – In-person watching
4. **Indirect Observation** – Through traces (e.g., web analytics, footfall counters)

**When to Use:**
- When respondents cannot or will not report accurately (e.g., children's preferences)
- When behavioral data is more reliable than stated data

---

### C. Experimental Research Technique

The "gold standard" for **causal research**. Involves manipulating one variable (independent) to observe its effect on another (dependent) while controlling other variables.

**Key Terminology:**
- **Independent Variable (IV):** The one manipulated (e.g., price level)
- **Dependent Variable (DV):** The one measured (e.g., sales volume)
- **Control Group:** Group NOT exposed to the manipulation
- **Test Group:** Group exposed to the manipulation

**Experimental Designs:**

| Design | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-test / Post-test Control Group** | Both groups measured before & after | Classic A/B testing |
| **After-Only Control Group** | Only post-experiment measurement | When pre-test is impossible |
| **Solomon Four-Group** | Combines both above; most rigorous | High-stakes decisions |
| **Factorial Design** | Tests multiple IVs simultaneously | Price + Packaging + Ad |

---

## 3.2 Survey Questionnaire Design and Drafting – Exhaustive Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a heavily tested area in KTU 2024.** Master this for guaranteed marks.

### Step 1: Define Survey Objectives
Before drafting a single question, answer:
- What decisions will this survey support?
- What information is required?
- Who will use the results?

### Step 2: Decide Data Collection Method
- Online, telephone, face-to-face, mail?
- *The method dictates question design.*

### Step 3: Determine Question Content and Type

**A. Structured (Closed-Ended) Questions:**

| Type | Example |
|---|---|
| **Dichotomous** | "Do you own a smartphone? Yes / No" |
| **Multiple Choice (Single)** | "Which brand do you prefer? A / B / C" |
| **Multiple Choice (Multiple)** | "Which features matter? (Select all that apply)" |
| **Likert Scale** | "Rate the service from 1 (Poor) to 5 (Excellent)" |
| **Semantic Differential** | "Reliable ___:___:___:___:___ Unreliable" |
| **Ranking Scale** | "Rank these 5 features in order of importance" |
| **Rating Scale** | "How likely are you to recommend us? 0–10 (NPS)" |

**B. Unstructured (Open-Ended) Questions:**

| Type | Example |
|---|---|
| **Fully Open** | "What do you think about our new product?" |
| **Verbal Association** | "What words come to mind when you hear 'Apple'?" |
| **Sentence Completion** | "I prefer brand X because ___" |
| **Story Completion** | "Imagine you used our product; describe the experience" |
| **Picture Completion** | Show a cartoon, ask what the character is thinking |

### Step 4: Word the Questions Properly

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall: Worded questions cost 30% of marks in question design questions.**

**Rules of Question Wording:**

1. **Avoid Double-Barreled Questions**
   - ❌ "Is our service fast and friendly?" (Two questions in one)
   - ✅ Split into two questions.

2. **Avoid Leading Questions**
   - ❌ "Don't you agree that our product is the best?"
   - ✅ "How would you rate our product compared to competitors?"

3. **Avoid Jargon / Technical Terms**
   - ❌ "What is your perception of our UI/UX optimization?"
   - ✅ "How easy is our app to use?"

4. **Avoid Vague Quantifiers**
   - ❌ "Do you often shop online?"
   - ✅ "How many times did you shop online last month? ___ times"

5. **Avoid Negative Wording**
   - ❌ "Are you unsatisfied with our service?"
   - ✅ "How satisfied are you with our service?"

6. **Avoid Hypothetical Questions**
   - ❌ "Would you buy a flying car for ₹20 lakhs?"
   - ✅ Better: Use conjoint analysis for hypothetical products.

7. **Use Simple Language**
   - Target: 8th-grade reading level for general consumers.

### Step 5: Determine Question Sequence / Flow

**The Funnel Approach:**

1. **Opening Questions** (Easy, non-threatening)
   - "How often do you buy groceries?"

2. **Qualifying Questions** (Filter respondents)
   - "Have you purchased our product in the last 6 months?" (If No → Skip remaining)

3. **Body Questions** (Main substantive content)
   - Attitude, behavior, awareness questions

4. **Difficult / Sensitive Questions** (Towards the end)
   - Income, personal habits, demographics

5. **Classification / Demographic Questions** (Last)
   - Age, gender, income, education

### Step 6: Physical Layout & Form Design

- Use clear instructions in **bold italics**
- Pre-code response categories
- Use a logical grid/table format
- Allow space for open-ended responses
- Include "Thank You" and confidentiality assurance
- Pilot test with 20–30 respondents before full launch

### Step 7: Pre-Test (Pilot Testing)
- Test for clarity, time taken, ambiguity
- Calculate Barth Index for readability
- Revise based on feedback

### Step 8: Final Drafting and Administration

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Question Sequence Used in KTU Sample Papers:**
> 1. Screening/Qualifying → 2. Warm-up → 3. Awareness → 4. Usage → 5. Attitude → 6. Intention → 7. Demographics

---

## 3.3 Pricing Research – Step-by-Step Analytical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Pricing Research is the systematic process of gathering, analyzing, and interpreting data to determine the optimal price for a product or service that maximizes profitability while delivering customer value.*

### Why Pricing Research is Critical
- Price is the **only element of the marketing mix that generates revenue**; all others are costs.
- A 1% price increase typically boosts operating profit by ~8–11% (McKinsey research).
- Wrong pricing is among the top 3 reasons for product failure.

### Step 1: Identify Pricing Objectives

| Objective | Description |
|---|---|
| **Profit Maximization** | Set price to maximize total profit |
| **Sales Growth / Volume** | Set price to maximize units sold |
| **Market Share** | Set price to gain market share (penetration) |
| **Survival** | Set price to cover variable costs in tough times |
| **Prestige / Skimming** | High price to signal premium quality |
| **Status Quo** | Match competitor pricing |

### Step 2: Determine Demand
- Estimate **Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)**:

$$E_p = \frac{\%\Delta Q_d}{\%\Delta P}$$

- **Inelastic demand** ($E_p < 1$): Price increase raises total revenue (e.g., petrol).
- **Elastic demand** ($E_p > 1$): Price decrease raises total revenue (e.g., restaurant food).
- **Unit elastic** ($E_p = 1$): Revenue constant.

### Step 3: Estimate Costs
- **Fixed Costs** (FC): Don't vary with output (rent, salaries)
- **Variable Costs** (VC): Vary with output (raw materials, packaging)
- **Total Cost (TC):** $TC = FC + VC$
- **Break-Even Volume:** $\text{BEV} = \frac{FC}{P - AVC}$

### Step 4: Analyze Competitors' Prices
- Direct competitors' pricing
- Substitute products
- Price-positioning map

### Step 5: Select a Pricing Method

| Method | Formula / Logic | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost-Plus Pricing** | $P = TC + \text{Markup}\%$ | Manufacturing, B2B |
| **Break-Even Pricing** | $P = \frac{FC}{Q} + AVC$ | Startups |
| **Value-Based Pricing** | Price set by perceived value | Premium brands (Apple, Tesla) |
| **Competition-Based Pricing** | Match or beat competitor | Commodities |
| **Dynamic Pricing** | Real-time algorithm-based price | Airlines, Uber, Ola |
| **Penetration Pricing** | Low initial price | New market entry |
| **Skimming Pricing** | High initial price | Innovators, luxury goods |
| **Psychological Pricing** | ₹999 vs ₹1000 | Retail |
| **Bundle Pricing** | Combined product price | Telecom plans |

### Step 6: Pricing Research Methods

1. **Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter (PSM)**
   - Four questions to identify:
     - "Too cheap" (lower threshold)
     - "Cheap / Good value"
     - "Expensive / Not a bargain"
     - "Too expensive" (upper threshold)
   - Intersection points → Optimal Price Range

2. **Conjoint Analysis**
   - Decomposes consumer preferences into utility values for product attributes.
   - Used for trade-off analysis.

3. **Gabor-Granger Method**
   - Asks willingness-to-buy at various price points.
   - Identifies revenue-maximizing price.

4. **Brand-Price Trade-Off (BPTO)**
   - Tests brand preference under different price scenarios.

5. **Monadic / Paired Comparison Tests**
   - Single product test vs. pair-wise test.

### Step 7: Finalize Price
- Consider legal, ethical, and image factors.
- Launch, monitor, and adjust.

---

## 3.4 Media Research – Detailed Analytical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Media Research is the systematic study of media audiences, vehicles, and effectiveness to optimize marketing communication budgets and maximize reach, frequency, and impact.*

### Objectives of Media Research
1. Identify the **target audience** and their media habits
2. Select the **right media mix** (TV, Print, Digital, Radio, OOH)
3. Determine **optimal reach and frequency**
4. Measure **advertising effectiveness** (pre & post)
5. Optimize **media budget allocation**
6. Track **competitor media spending**

### Key Media Research Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Reach** | % of target audience exposed at least once | Breadth |
| **Frequency** | Average exposures per reached person | Depth |
| **GRPs** | $\text{Reach} \times \text{Frequency}$ | Total advertising weight |
| **TRPs** (Television) | Audience % for a specific time slot | TV-specific GRP |
| **CPRP** | $\frac{\text{Media Cost}}{\text{GRPs}}$ | Cost efficiency |
| **OTS (Opportunities to See)** | Total exposures delivered | Reach × Frequency × Population |
| **Effective Frequency** | Minimum exposures needed for impact | Usually 3+ |
| **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $\frac{\text{Brand Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Competitive presence |
| **Share of Market (SOM)** | $\frac{\text{Brand Sales}}{\text{Total Category Sales}} \times 100$ | Market performance |

### Major Media Research Tools in India

1. **BARC (Broadcast Audience Research Council)**
   - TV viewership data (TRPs)
   - Uses BAR-O-Meters in 44,000+ households

2. **TAM (Television Audience Measurement)**
   - Historical player; merged with BARC

3. **IRS (Indian Readership Survey)**
   - Print media readership (newspapers, magazines)

4. **RAM (Radio Audience Measurement)**
   - Radio listenership

5. **Nielsen (AdEx)** – Advertising Expenditure data
6. **comScore** – Digital audience measurement
7. **Google Analytics, Meta Insights** – Digital MR

### Media Mix Models (MMM)
- Statistical models to attribute sales to each media channel.
- Use regression: $\text{Sales} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{TV} + \beta_2 X_{Print} + \beta_3 X_{Digital} + \epsilon$

### Advertising Effectiveness Research

| Type | Timing | What It Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Testing** | Before launch | Recall, comprehension, persuasion |
| **Concurrent Testing** | During campaign | In-flight optimization |
| **Post-Testing** | After campaign | Brand awareness, attitude, sales lift |

**Common Pre-Testing Methods:**
- **DAR (Day-After-Recall)**
- **Starch Readership Test** (Print)
- **Gallup-Robinson Impact Tests**
- **Eye-Tracking Studies**

---

## 3.5 Qualitative Research – Exhaustive Analytical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024):**
> *Qualitative Research is a non-numerical, exploratory research method used to gain an in-depth understanding of underlying reasons, opinions, and motivations. It provides insights into the problem and helps develop hypotheses for quantitative testing.*

### Philosophy
- **Interpretivist** paradigm (vs. Positivist for Quantitative)
- Looks for **"Why" and "How"** rather than "How many"
- Small samples, depth over breadth
- Researcher is the primary instrument

### Six Major Qualitative Research Techniques

#### 1. Focus Group Discussion (FGD)
- **Definition:** A facilitated group discussion with 6–10 participants, led by a moderator, lasting 1–2 hours.
- **Process:**
  1. Recruitment (screening)
  2. Setting (typically with one-way mirror)
  3. Discussion guide preparation
  4. Moderation
  5. Recording (audio/video)
  6. Transcribing and analysis
- **Use:** Concept testing, ad pre-testing, exploratory research.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention "6–10 participants, homogeneous yet diverse" for full marks.

#### 2. Depth Interview (In-Depth Interview / IDI)
- **Definition:** A one-on-one, unstructured or semi-structured interview lasting 30–60 minutes.
- **Types:**
  - **Structured** (fixed questions)
  - **Semi-structured** (flexible guide)
  - **Unstructured** (free conversation)
- **Techniques:** Laddering, Symbolic Analysis, Critical Incident Technique
- **Use:** Sensitive topics, B2B, elite respondents.

#### 3. Projective Techniques
> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love this section. Projective techniques are psychological tools that reveal **hidden or subconscious** attitudes.

| Technique | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Word Association** | First word that comes to mind | "Coffee" → "Energy" |
| **Sentence Completion** | Complete a sentence | "People who buy Tesla are ___" |
| **Story Completion** | Complete a story | "A woman walks into a Maruti showroom..." |
| **Picture / Cartoon Test** | Interpret a drawing | "What is the man thinking?" |
| **Thematic Apperception Test (TAT)** | Tell a story about an ambiguous picture | Used in psychology & MR |
| **Role Playing** | Act out a buying scenario | Simulate supermarket experience |
| **Third-Person Technique** | "Why do MOST people buy X?" (Easier to answer than personal) | Avoids social desirability bias |
| **Personification** | "If our brand were a person, describe it" | Brand personality research |

#### 4. Ethnographic Research
- Immersion in the consumer's natural environment.
- Researchers live with consumers, observe routines.
- *Example:* P&G's "Living It" program – researchers lived with families for weeks.

#### 5. Case Study Method
- In-depth study of a single individual, group, or organization.
- Provides rich contextual data.

#### 6. Online Qualitative Research
- **Online Focus Groups (Bulletin Board FGs)**
- **Video Chat Interviews**
- **Mobile Ethnography** (participants record daily vlogs)
- **Social Media Listening**

### Qualitative vs. Quantitative – KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Explore, understand | Measure, count, test |
| **Data Type** | Non-numerical (text, images) | Numerical (scores, ratings) |
| **Sample Size** | Small (10–30) | Large (200+) |
| **Analysis** | Thematic, interpretive | Statistical |
| **Output** | Hypotheses, insights | Validated conclusions, generalizations |
| **Researcher Role** | Subjective, immersed | Objective, detached |
| **Cost** | Lower (per study) | Higher |
| **Time** | Days to weeks | Months |

### Limitations of Qualitative Research
1. **Subjectivity** – Results depend on researcher's interpretation.
2. **Small Sample** – Cannot be generalized to the population.
3. **Time-Intensive Analysis** – Transcription and coding take weeks.
4. **Respondent Bias** – Social desirability and moderator influence.
5. **No Statistical Validity** – Cannot test hypotheses rigorously.

### Software Tools for Qualitative Analysis
- **NVivo**
- **ATLAS.ti**
- **MAXQDA**
- **Dedoose**
- (Manual coding for small studies)
